parts.content: Identify the direction of electron flow in a zinc–copper voltaic cell.
parts.markscheme:
From zinc to copper
<marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Identify the metals
steps.content: To determine the direction of electron flow, we first identify the two metals used in the electrodes: zinc and copper.

steps.title: Compare reactivity
steps.content:
Using the **activity series**, we compare the relative reactivity of the two metals. Zinc ($Zn$) is more reactive than copper ($Cu$). 

This means zinc has a higher tendency to lose electrons (undergo oxidation) compared to copper.

steps.title: Determine the oxidation site
steps.content:
In a voltaic cell, oxidation occurs at the **anode**. Since zinc is more reactive, the zinc electrode acts as the anode, where it releases electrons according to the half-equation:

$$Zn(s) \rightarrow Zn^{2+}(aq) + 2e^-$$

steps.title: Trace the electron flow
steps.content:
Electrons are produced at the zinc anode and travel through the external circuit (the wire) to the copper cathode, where reduction takes place. 

Therefore, the electrons flow **from zinc to copper**.

steps.title: Identify the goal
steps.content: To find the **direction of electron flow**, we need to determine which metal acts as the **anode** (where oxidation happens) and which acts as the **cathode** (where reduction happens). In a voltaic cell, electrons always flow through the external circuit from the anode to the cathode.

steps.title: Consult the Data Booklet
steps.content:
Using the IB Data Booklet, we find the standard reduction potentials ($E^{\ominus}$) for the two half-reactions involved:

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Zn}^{2+}(\text{aq}) + 2e^- &\rightleftharpoons \text{Zn}(\text{s}) & E^{\ominus} = -0.76\text{ V} \\ \text{Cu}^{2+}(\text{aq}) + 2e^- &\rightleftharpoons \text{Cu}(\text{s}) & E^{\ominus} = +0.34\text{ V} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Identify Anode and Cathode
steps.content:
The species with the **more negative** electrode potential is more easily oxidized. Comparing our values:

$$-0.76\text{ V} < +0.34\text{ V}$$

Zinc has the more negative potential, so it undergoes oxidation at the **anode**:
$$\text{Zn}(\text{s}) \rightarrow \text{Zn}^{2+}(\text{aq}) + 2e^-$$

Copper has the more positive potential, so its ions undergo reduction at the **cathode**:
$$\text{Cu}^{2+}(\text{aq}) + 2e^- \rightarrow \text{Cu}(\text{s})$$

steps.title: Determine flow direction
steps.content: Since electrons are produced at the anode (Zinc) and consumed at the cathode (Copper), they must flow from the zinc electrode to the copper electrode through the connecting wire.

steps.title: Final Answer
steps.content:
The direction of electron flow is from **zinc to copper** (or Zn to Cu).

In the IB markscheme, you earn **1 mark** for correctly identifying this specific direction.

parts.content: State the species reduced at the cathode.
parts.markscheme:
$\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}$ is reduced
<marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Use the 'RED CAT' mnemonic
steps.content:
To determine what happens at each electrode in an electrochemical cell, we use the mnemonic **RED CAT**. 

This stands for:
- **RED**uction occurs at the **CAT**hode.
- (**AN OX** is also helpful: **AN**ode is where **OX**idation occurs).

Since the question asks for the species reduced at the cathode, we know we are looking for the reactant in the reduction half-equation.

highlights.text: State the species reduced at the cathode.

steps.title: Identify the reduction half-reaction
steps.content:
Reduction is the gain of electrons. In a standard voltaic cell involving copper and zinc (a common example in IB Chemistry), the copper half-reaction at the cathode involves copper(II) ions gaining electrons to form copper metal:

$$\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}(aq) + 2e^- \rightarrow \mathrm{Cu}(s)$$

This confirms that the species being reduced is the one on the reactant side of this equation.

steps.title: State the final species
steps.content:
Based on the reduction process at the cathode, the species gaining electrons is the copper(II) ion.

The final answer is $\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}$.

steps.title: Link reduction to the cathode
steps.content: In electrochemistry, we use the mnemonic **RED CAT** to remember that **red**uction always occurs at the **cat**hode in both voltaic and electrolytic cells. To find out which species is reduced, we need to determine which half-cell reaction is most likely to gain electrons.

highlights.text: State the species reduced at the cathode.

steps.title: Reference standard electrode potentials
steps.content:
To decide which reaction happens at the cathode, we look at the **Standard Electrode Potentials** ($E^{\ominus}$) in the IB Data Booklet. For a typical voltaic cell (like the Zinc-Copper cell), the half-reactions are:

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Reaction 1: } & \mathrm{Cu}^{2+}(\text{aq}) + 2e^{-} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{Cu}(\text{s}) & E^{\ominus} = +0.34\text{ V} \\ \text{Reaction 2: } & \mathrm{Zn}^{2+}(\text{aq}) + 2e^{-} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{Zn}(\text{s}) & E^{\ominus} = -0.76\text{ V} \end{aligned}$$

These values represent the tendency of a species to be reduced.

steps.title: Compare $E^{\ominus}$ values
steps.content:
The species with the **more positive** (or less negative) $E^{\ominus}$ value is the stronger oxidizing agent and will undergo reduction at the cathode. 

Comparing our values:
$$+0.34\text{ V} > -0.76\text{ V}$$

Since the copper half-reaction has the more positive potential, it will proceed as a reduction.

steps.title: Identify the reduced species
steps.content:
Because the reduction reaction is $\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}(\text{aq}) + 2e^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{Cu}(\text{s})$, the specific chemical species gaining electrons is the copper(II) ion.

Therefore, the species reduced at the cathode is **$\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}$**.

parts.content: Explain why the flow of electrons occurs in that direction.
parts.markscheme:
- Zinc is more reactive and more readily loses electrons.
<marks>1 mark</marks>
- Electrons flow from the more reactive to the less reactive metal.
<marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Compare metal reactivity
steps.content: In a voltaic cell, the movement of electrons is driven by the difference in chemical reactivity between two metals. A more reactive metal has a higher tendency to lose electrons than a less reactive one.

steps.title: Identify the oxidation source
steps.content:
Zinc is more reactive than metals like copper, meaning it is a stronger reducing agent and more readily loses electrons. Because it loses electrons, the zinc electrode acts as the **anode** where oxidation occurs:

$$Zn(s) \rightarrow Zn^{2+}(aq) + 2e^-$$

highlights.text: Zinc is more reactive and more readily loses electrons.

steps.title: Determine electron flow direction
steps.content:
Since electrons are being released at the more reactive metal (the anode), they must flow through the external circuit toward the less reactive metal (the cathode). 

Therefore, the flow is always from the more reactive metal to the less reactive metal.

highlights.text: Electrons flow from the more reactive to the less reactive metal.

steps.title: Compare standard reduction potentials
steps.content:
To explain the flow of electrons, we look at the **standard reduction potentials** ($E^{\ominus}$) in the IB data booklet. In a voltaic cell (like a Zinc-Copper cell), we compare the tendency of each half-cell to gain electrons:

$$\begin{aligned} E^{\ominus}(\text{Zn}^{2+}/\text{Zn}) &= -0.76 \text{ V} \\ E^{\ominus}(\text{Cu}^{2+}/\text{Cu}) &= +0.34 \text{ V} \end{aligned}$$

The species with the **more negative** (or less positive) potential has the greatest tendency to lose electrons and undergo oxidation.

steps.title: Identify the more reactive metal
steps.content:
Based on these potentials, Zinc is the more reactive metal. Because its $E^{\ominus}$ is more negative, it acts as the **reducing agent** and loses electrons more readily than Copper.

$$\text{Zn(s)} \rightarrow \text{Zn}^{2+}\text{(aq)} + 2e^-$$

steps.title: Define the electron flow
steps.content:
In a voltaic cell, oxidation occurs at the **anode** (the more reactive metal). The released electrons then move through the external circuit toward the **cathode** (the less reactive metal) where reduction takes place. 

Therefore, the flow occurs in this direction because electrons always move from the more reactive metal (which loses them) to the less reactive metal.

parts.content: Write the oxidation half-equation for iron forming $Fe^{2+}\text{(aq)}$.
parts.markscheme: $Fe\text{(s)} \to Fe^{2+}\text{(aq)} + 2e^-$ <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Identify reactant and product
steps.content:
To write the oxidation half-equation, we first identify the species involved based on the question. We are starting with **iron metal** and forming **iron(II) ions**.

This is a classic example of Topic **R3.2: Electron transfer reactions**, where oxidation is defined as the loss of electrons.

steps.title: Write the skeletal equation
steps.content:
We place the iron metal on the reactant side and the iron(II) ion on the product side. Don't forget to include the state symbols: $(s)$ for solid metal and $(aq)$ for the aqueous ion.

$$Fe(s) \to Fe^{2+}(aq)$$

steps.title: Balance the charge
steps.content:
In a balanced half-equation, the total charge on both sides must be equal. 
- The left side has a charge of $0$.
- The right side has a charge of $+2$.

To balance this, we add two electrons ($2e^-$) to the product side. This represents the iron atom losing electrons to become a cation.

$$Fe(s) \to Fe^{2+}(aq) + 2e^-$$

steps.title: Identify the process
steps.content:
The question asks for the **oxidation** half-equation of iron forming $Fe^{2+}$. 

Recall the mnemonic **OIL RIG**: **O**xidation **I**s **L**oss of electrons. This means electrons will appear on the product side of our equation.

steps.title: Locate the Data Booklet
steps.content:
In the IB Chemistry Data Booklet, navigate to the table of **Standard electrode potentials**. 

All half-equations in this table are written as **reductions** (gaining electrons). Look for the equation involving iron metal ($Fe$) and iron(II) ions ($Fe^{2+}$).

steps.title: Extract and reverse
steps.content:
In the data booklet, you will find the following reduction half-equation:

$$Fe^{2+}(\text{aq}) + 2e^- \rightleftharpoons Fe(\text{s})$$

Since we need the **oxidation** half-equation, we must reverse this process so that iron metal is the reactant and electrons are lost (produced).

steps.title: State the final equation
steps.content:
By reversing the equation and ensuring states are included, we obtain the oxidation half-equation for iron:

$$Fe(\text{s}) \to Fe^{2+}(\text{aq}) + 2e^-$$

parts.content: Write the reduction half-equation for silver ions forming silver metal.
parts.markscheme: $Ag^+\text{(aq)} + e^- \to Ag\text{(s)}$ <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Identify reactant and product
steps.content:
From the question, we are told that iron is placed in a **silver nitrate solution** and a **grey solid** forms. In an aqueous solution, silver nitrate exists as silver ions ($Ag^+$). The grey solid formed on the iron surface is silver metal ($Ag$). 

So, our transition is:
$$Ag^+ 	o Ag$$

steps.title: Determine oxidation states
steps.content:
To find out how many electrons are involved, we look at the change in oxidation states:
- The oxidation state of a monatomic ion is equal to its charge. For $Ag^+$, it is $+1$.
- The oxidation state of an element in its standard state (like $Ag$ metal) is $0$.

The oxidation state decreases from $+1$ to $0$, which confirms this is a **reduction** process (gain of electrons).

steps.title: Balance the charge
steps.content:
We need to balance the charge by adding electrons ($e^-$). Since the reactant side has a charge of $+1$ and the product side is $0$, we must add one electron to the reactant side to make the total charge on both sides equal to zero.

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Total reactant charge: } &+1 + (-1) = 0 \\ \text{Total product charge: } &0 \end{aligned}$$

This gives us the final half-equation including state symbols:
$$Ag^+\text{(aq)} + e^- \to Ag\text{(s)}$$

steps.title: Identify the process
steps.content: The question asks for the **reduction half-equation** for silver ions. In redox chemistry, reduction is defined as the **gain of electrons**. We are starting with silver ions in solution and forming solid silver metal.

highlights.text: reduction half-equation for silver ions forming silver metal

steps.title: Consult the Data Booklet
steps.content:
To find the specific equation, we look at the **Standard electrode potentials** table in the IB Chemistry Data Booklet. This table lists various half-equations, all written in the form of a reduction (species + electrons $\to$ product). 

We need to find the entry where silver ions ($Ag^+$) gain electrons to become silver metal ($Ag$).

steps.title: Write the half-equation
steps.content:
From the data table, we locate the half-equation for silver. Since silver ions have a $1+$ charge, they must gain one electron ($e^-$) to form a neutral silver atom. 

Don't forget to include the state symbols: $(aq)$ for the ions in solution and $(s)$ for the solid metal formed.

$$Ag^+(aq) + e^- \to Ag(s)$$ 

This matches the entry in the standard electrode potential table exactly.

parts.content: Combine the two half-equations to write the overall ionic redox equation.
parts.markscheme:
- Identify the correct species and states <marks>A1</marks>
- Balance the equation for both mass and charge: $Fe\text{(s)} + 2Ag^+\text{(aq)} \to Fe^{2+}\text{(aq)} + 2Ag\text{(s)}$ <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Identify the oxidation half-reaction
steps.content:
First, we look at the iron. The iron metal ($Fe$) is oxidized to form iron(II) ions ($Fe^{2+}$). In this process, each iron atom loses two electrons:

$$Fe(s) \to Fe^{2+}(aq) + 2e^-$$

steps.title: Identify the reduction half-reaction
steps.content:
Next, we look at the silver. Silver ions ($Ag^+$) from the silver nitrate solution gain electrons to form the grey solid, which is silver metal ($Ag$). Each silver ion gains one electron:

$$Ag^+(aq) + e^- \to Ag(s)$$ 

This is the reduction half-reaction because electrons are gained.

steps.title: Equalize the electrons
steps.content:
In a redox reaction, the number of electrons lost must equal the number of electrons gained. 

Since the iron oxidation loses **2 electrons** and the silver reduction only gains **1 electron**, we must multiply the entire silver half-equation by 2:

$$2Ag^+(aq) + 2e^- \to 2Ag(s)$$ 

Now, both equations involve 2 electrons.

steps.title: Combine the half-equations
steps.content:
Now we add the two half-equations together. The electrons on both sides cancel out, leaving us with the overall ionic equation:

$$\begin{aligned} Fe(s) &\to Fe^{2+}(aq) + 2e^- \\ 2Ag^+(aq) + 2e^- &\to 2Ag(s) \\ \hline Fe(s) + 2Ag^+(aq) &\to Fe^{2+}(aq) + 2Ag(s) \end{aligned}$$

To earn full marks, ensure you have included the correct state symbols ($s$ for solids and $aq$ for ions in solution) and that both the atoms and the total charges are balanced.

steps.title: Identify oxidation states
steps.content:
First, we assign oxidation states to all atoms in the reaction. According to the rules covered in **Topic S3.1.6 Oxidation states**, elements in their natural state have an oxidation number of 0, and simple ions have an oxidation number equal to their charge.

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Reactants: } & \text{Fe (solid metal)} = 0 \\ & \text{Ag}^+ \text{ (ion)} = +1 \\ \text{Products: } & \text{Fe}^{2+} \text{ (ion)} = +2 \\ & \text{Ag (solid metal)} = 0 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Determine oxidation state changes
steps.content:
Next, we calculate the change in oxidation state for each element to see how many electrons are moving around.

- **Iron (Fe):** Changes from $0$ to $+2$. This is an increase of $2$, meaning it is oxidized.
- **Silver (Ag):** Changes from $+1$ to $0$. This is a decrease of $1$, meaning it is reduced.

steps.title: Equalize increase and decrease
steps.content:
In a redox reaction, the total increase in oxidation state must equal the total decrease. 

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Increase (Fe): } & +2 \\ \text{Decrease (Ag): } & -1 \end{aligned}$$

To balance these, we need two Silver atoms for every one Iron atom. This ensures the total decrease ($2 \times -1 = -2$) cancels out the total increase ($+2$).

steps.title: Write the balanced equation
steps.content:
Finally, we combine these species into the overall ionic equation. We must include the state symbols as specified in the markscheme to earn full marks.

$$Fe(s) + 2Ag^+(aq) \to Fe^{2+}(aq) + 2Ag(s)$$

Notice that the charge is balanced: the reactant side has a total charge of $+2$ ($2 \times +1$) and the product side also has a total charge of $+2$.

highlights.text: Identify the correct species and states
Balance the equation for both mass and charge

parts.content: Identify the species oxidized and the species reduced in this reaction.
parts.markscheme: - Identify both species: $Fe$ / iron is oxidized **AND** $Ag^+$ / silver ion is reduced <marks>A1</marks>

parts.content: Suggest why this reaction occurs spontaneously, based on relative reactivity.
parts.markscheme: - State that iron is more reactive than silver **OR** iron is higher in the activity series <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Identify the reaction type
steps.content: When a solid metal is placed into a solution of another metal's salt and a change is observed, we are looking at a **single displacement reaction**. For this reaction to occur spontaneously, the solid metal must be more "active" than the metal ions in the solution.

highlights.text: iron metal is placed into a solution of silver nitrate

steps.title: Compare activity series positions
steps.content:
The **activity series** (found in your IB Data Booklet) ranks metals by their reactivity. Metals higher in the series have a greater tendency to lose electrons (oxidize) and displace metal ions lower in the series.

Looking at the series, we find that iron ($Fe$) is positioned significantly higher than silver ($Ag$).

steps.title: State the reactivity relationship
steps.content:
Because iron is higher in the activity series, it is more reactive than silver. This means iron will spontaneously transfer electrons to the silver ions ($Ag^{+}$), causing the iron to dissolve and solid silver to precipitate out as the grey solid observed.

$$\text{Fe}(s) + 2\text{AgNO}_3(aq) \rightarrow \text{Fe(NO}_3)_2(aq) + 2\text{Ag}(s)$$

According to the markscheme, simply stating that iron is more reactive is enough to earn the mark.

steps.title: Identify the process
steps.content:
To understand why this reaction occurs spontaneously, we need to look at what is happening to the particles. Iron metal ($Fe$) is reacting with silver ions ($Ag^+$) from the silver nitrate solution. This is a redox reaction where iron is being oxidized and silver ions are being reduced:

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Oxidation:} & \quad Fe(s) \rightarrow Fe^{2+}(aq) + 2e^- \\ \text{Reduction:} & \quad Ag^+(aq) + e^- \rightarrow Ag(s) \end{aligned}$$

This displacement occurs because one metal is "better" at losing electrons than the other.

highlights.text: iron metal is placed into a solution of silver nitrate

steps.title: Consult electrode potentials
steps.content:
In your IB Data Booklet, you can find the **Standard Electrode Potentials** table. We look for the reduction potentials ($E^\ominus$) for both iron and silver:

$$\begin{aligned} Fe^{2+}(aq) + 2e^- &\rightleftharpoons Fe(s) & E^\ominus = -0.44\text{ V} \\ Ag^+(aq) + e^- &\rightleftharpoons Ag(s) & E^\ominus = +0.80\text{ V} \end{aligned}$$

The $E^\ominus$ value tells us how easily a species is reduced. A more positive value means the species is a stronger oxidizing agent (it wants to be reduced), while a more negative value means the species is a stronger reducing agent (it wants to be oxidized).

steps.title: Compare relative reactivity
steps.content:
By comparing the values, we see that iron has a significantly more negative $E^\ominus$ value ($-0.44\text{ V}$) than silver ($+0.80\text{ V}$). 

In terms of reactivity, a more negative electrode potential indicates a more reactive metal. This means iron is a stronger reducing agent than silver and has a greater tendency to lose electrons. 

Because the iron is more reactive, it successfully displaces the silver ions from the solution, leading to a spontaneous reaction.

gdc.instructions: Subtract the more negative potential from the more positive potential.

ti84.instructions: Enter the reduction potential of the cathode minus the reduction potential of the anode.

desmos.instructions: Calculate the difference between the reduction and oxidation potentials.

calculator.description: Calculate the standard cell potential (E_cell) to confirm spontaneity.

steps.title: Final Conclusion
steps.content:
The reaction is spontaneous because **iron is more reactive than silver**. 

You can also state this in terms of the activity series: iron is higher in the activity series than silver, allowing it to displace silver ions from the solution.

highlights.text: State that iron is more reactive than silver

parts.content: Identify the product formed at the cathode during this electrolysis.
parts.markscheme:
- State the name or formula of the product: magnesium / $Mg$ / $Mg(l)$ <marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

steps.title: Identify ions in electrolyte
steps.content:
To understand what happens during electrolysis, we first need to look at what's inside the container. When we have **molten** magnesium chloride, the ionic lattice has broken down, allowing the ions to move freely. 

The compound dissociates into its constituent ions:
$$\text{MgCl}_2(l) \rightarrow \text{Mg}^{2+}(l) + 2\text{Cl}^-(l)$$

This falls under **Topic R3.2: Electron transfer reactions**, specifically the study of electrolytic cells.

steps.title: Determine ion migration
steps.content:
In an electrolytic cell, the power source pushes electrons toward the **cathode**, making it the **negative electrode**. 

Opposites attract! The positive magnesium ions (cations) migrate toward the negative cathode. 

$$\text{Mg}^{2+} \text{ (positive cation)} \rightarrow \text{Negative Cathode}$$

steps.title: Apply reduction principle
steps.content:
Once the $\text{Mg}^{2+}$ ions reach the cathode, they undergo **reduction**. Remember the mnemonic **OIL RIG**: Reduction Is Gain of electrons.

The magnesium ions gain electrons from the electrode surface to form neutral magnesium atoms:

$$\text{Mg}^{2+}(l) + 2e^- \rightarrow \text{Mg}(l)$$

Because the system is heated, the magnesium is initially produced in its liquid state.

steps.title: State the final product
steps.content:
Based on the reduction half-equation at the cathode, the product formed is magnesium metal. 

In your IB exam, you can provide either the name or the chemical symbol to earn the mark.

steps.title: Identify ions in electrolyte
steps.content:
First, we need to identify the ions present in the molten salt. Molten magnesium chloride consists of magnesium ions and chloride ions. Since it is molten (and not an aqueous solution), there is no water to compete at the electrodes.

$$\text{MgCl}_2(l) \rightarrow \text{Mg}^{2+}(l) + 2\text{Cl}^-(l)$$

This concept is part of **Topic R3.2: Electron transfer reactions**, which covers the behavior of ions in electrolytic cells.

steps.title: Determine cathode polarity
steps.content: In an electrolytic cell, the cathode is the negative electrode. Because opposite charges attract, the positive magnesium ions ($Mg^{2+}$) will migrate toward the cathode.

highlights.text: Identify the product formed at the cathode

steps.title: Deduce the half-equation
steps.content:
At the cathode, reduction always occurs (remember the mnemonic **RED CAT**). Reduction is the gain of electrons. 

We can deduce the half-equation by showing the magnesium ions gaining electrons to form neutral magnesium atoms:

$$\text{Mg}^{2+} + 2e^- \rightarrow \text{Mg}$$

Since the process happens at high temperatures (above the melting point), the magnesium is typically produced in liquid form, $\text{Mg}(l)$.

steps.title: Final product identification
steps.content:
Based on the reduction half-equation, the product formed at the cathode is **magnesium metal** (or simply **magnesium**). 

In the exam, you can provide either the name or the chemical symbol.

parts.content: Write the half-equation for the formation of this product.
parts.markscheme:
- Identify the correct species: $Mg^{2+}$ and $e^-$ on the left, $Mg$ on the right <marks>A1</marks>
- Write the balanced equation: $Mg^{2+} + 2e^- \to Mg(l)$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

<note>Accept state symbol (l) for magnesium, but do not penalize its absence unless specified.</note>

steps.title: Identify the ions present
steps.content:
In its molten state, magnesium chloride ($MgCl_2$) dissociates into its constituent ions. Since magnesium is in Group 2 of the periodic table, it loses two electrons to achieve a stable electron configuration, forming the magnesium ion, $Mg^{2+}$.

This is covered in **Structure 2.1: The ionic model**, which explains the formation of ions from binary compounds.

steps.title: Apply reduction logic
steps.content:
To extract magnesium metal ($Mg$) from these ions, the $Mg^{2+}$ ions must travel to the **cathode** (the negative electrode). At the cathode, the ions gain electrons to become neutral atoms. 

In chemistry, the gain of electrons is defined as **reduction**. Since we are forming a neutral metal atom from a $2+$ ion, the magnesium ion must gain exactly two electrons to balance the charge.

steps.title: Construct the half-equation
steps.content:
We can now write the half-equation by placing the reactants (ions and electrons) on the left and the product (metal) on the right. 

$$\text{Mg}^{2+} + 2e^- \to \text{Mg}$$

According to the markscheme, you earn the first mark for identifying the correct species ($Mg^{2+}$, $e^-$, and $Mg$) and the second mark for balancing the equation correctly. While state symbols aren't strictly required unless asked for, using $(l)$ for liquid is appropriate here as the process is industrial and molten.

steps.title: Identify the magnesium species
steps.content:
In molten magnesium chloride, the compound exists as free-moving ions. We need to identify the form of magnesium in the electrolyte and the final product.

*   **Reactant:** Magnesium ions ($Mg^{2+}$) found in the molten $MgCl_2$.
*   **Product:** Magnesium metal ($Mg$) as specified in the question.

steps.title: Analyze oxidation states
steps.content:
To write the half-equation using oxidation state analysis, we determine the change in the oxidation number of magnesium:

1.  **In the reactant ($Mg^{2+}$):** The oxidation state is $+2$ (equal to the charge of the ion).
2.  **In the product ($Mg$):** The oxidation state of an element in its pure form is always $0$.

This decrease in oxidation state from $+2$ to $0$ confirms that magnesium is being **reduced**.

steps.title: Balance the charge
steps.content:
Since the oxidation state decreases by $2$ (from $+2$ to $0$), we must add $2$ electrons to the reactant side to balance the charge and account for the reduction process.

$$\text{Change} = 0 - (+2) = -2$$ 

This tells us that $2$ negative charges (electrons) are gained by each magnesium ion.

steps.title: Write the final equation
steps.content:
Combining the species and the electrons, we get the balanced half-equation. Because the electrolyte is molten (liquid), we can include the state symbol $(l)$ for the magnesium produced.

$$Mg^{2+} + 2e^{-} \to Mg(l)$$

According to the markscheme, you earn one mark for identifying the correct species ($Mg^{2+}$, $e^{-}$, and $Mg$) and a second mark for balancing the equation correctly.

highlights.text: Identify the correct species: Mg2+ and e- on the left, Mg on the right
Write the balanced equation: Mg2+ + 2e- → Mg(l)

steps.title: Identify the target species
steps.content: In the electrolysis of **molten** magnesium chloride, the salt is decomposed into its elements. Since magnesium is a metal, it is formed at the cathode through the reduction of magnesium ions. We need to find the specific half-equation that shows $Mg^{2+}$ ions gaining electrons to become magnesium metal.

steps.title: Consult the Data Booklet
steps.content: To find the exact stoichiometry and electron count, refer to the **Standard electrode potentials** table in your IB Chemistry Data Booklet. This table lists half-equations for many common ions. Search for the entry involving magnesium ($Mg$).

steps.title: Transcribe the reduction equation
steps.content:
From the data booklet, we find the reduction half-reaction. Because we are forming the product (magnesium metal) from the ions, we write it as a one-way reaction:

$$Mg^{2+} + 2e^- \to Mg$$

This shows that one magnesium ion ($Mg^{2+}$) requires two electrons ($2e^-$) to be neutralized into a magnesium atom ($Mg$).

steps.title: Finalize with state symbols
steps.content:
The question specifies the electrolyte is **molten** and heated above the melting point. Therefore, both the reactant ions and the product metal will be in the liquid state $(l)$. 

According to the markscheme, you earn one mark for the correct species ($Mg^{2+}$, $e^-$, and $Mg$) and one mark for the correctly balanced equation:

$$Mg^{2+}(l) + 2e^- \to Mg(l)$$ 

*(Note: State symbols are usually optional in IB redox equations unless the question specifically asks for them, but including them for molten processes is excellent practice!)*

highlights.text: Identify the correct species: $Mg^{2+}$ and $e^-$ on the left, $Mg$ on the right

parts.content: Explain why electrolysis of molten salts is considered a redox reaction, referring to both electrode processes.
parts.markscheme:
- Identify reduction at the cathode: $Mg^{2+}$ ions gain electrons / are reduced <marks>A1</marks>
- Identify oxidation at the anode: $Cl^-$ ions lose electrons / are oxidized <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

<note>Award 1 mark for identifying that both oxidation and reduction occur.</note>

steps.title: Define Redox via Electron Transfer
steps.content: To explain why this is a redox reaction, we look at the movement of electrons. In chemistry, **redox** is short for **red**uction-**ox**idation. A reaction is redox if one species loses electrons (oxidation) while another species gains those electrons (reduction). In the electrolysis of molten $MgCl_2$, the ions $Mg^{2+}$ and $Cl^-$ undergo these changes at the electrodes.

steps.title: Identify Reduction at the Cathode
steps.content:
At the negative electrode (**cathode**), the positive magnesium ions ($Mg^{2+}$) are attracted. Each $Mg^{2+}$ ion **gains two electrons** to become a neutral magnesium atom:

$$Mg^{2+} + 2e^- → Mg$$

Because the gain of electrons is defined as reduction, we say the magnesium ions are reduced. This earns the first mark.

highlights.text: Identify reduction at the cathode: $Mg^{2+}$ ions gain electrons / are reduced

steps.title: Identify Oxidation at the Anode
steps.content:
At the positive electrode (**anode**), the negative chloride ions ($Cl^-$) are attracted. Each chloride ion **loses an electron** to form chlorine atoms, which then pair up to form chlorine gas ($Cl_2$):

$$2Cl^- → Cl_2 + 2e^-$$

Because the loss of electrons is defined as oxidation, we say the chloride ions are oxidized. This earns the second mark.

highlights.text: Identify oxidation at the anode: $Cl^-$ ions lose electrons / are oxidized

steps.title: Summarize the Redox Process
steps.content: Since oxidation (loss of electrons) occurs at the anode and reduction (gain of electrons) occurs at the cathode simultaneously, the overall electrolysis process is a redox reaction. This concept is a fundamental part of **Topic R3.2: Electron transfer reactions** in your IB syllabus.

steps.title: Identify initial oxidation states
steps.content:
To explain the redox nature of this reaction, we first determine the oxidation states of the elements in the molten salt, $MgCl_2$. 

In the ionic compound $MgCl_2$, magnesium exists as $Mg^{2+}$ and chlorine as $Cl^-$. Following the rules for oxidation states:
- The oxidation state of $Mg$ in $Mg^{2+}$ is $+2$.
- The oxidation state of $Cl$ in $Cl^-$ is $-1$.

steps.title: Analyze the cathode process
steps.content:
At the negative electrode (cathode), $Mg^{2+}$ ions gain electrons to form liquid magnesium metal:

$$Mg^{2+} + 2e^- \rightarrow Mg(l)$$

The oxidation state of magnesium changes from **$+2$ to $0$** (elements in their standard state always have an oxidation state of $0$). A **decrease** in oxidation state signifies that **reduction** has occurred.

highlights.text: Identify reduction at the cathode: Mg2+ ions gain electrons / are reduced

steps.title: Analyze the anode process
steps.content:
At the positive electrode (anode), $Cl^-$ ions lose electrons to form chlorine gas:

$$2Cl^- \rightarrow Cl_2(g) + 2e^-$$

The oxidation state of chlorine changes from **$-1$ to $0$**. An **increase** in oxidation state signifies that **oxidation** has occurred.

highlights.text: Identify oxidation at the anode: Cl- ions lose electrons / are oxidized

steps.title: Define as redox reaction
steps.content:
A **redox** reaction is defined as a chemical process where both **red**uction and **ox**idation occur simultaneously. 

Because the oxidation state of magnesium decreases (reduction) and the oxidation state of chlorine increases (oxidation) during the electrolysis of $MgCl_2$, the process is classified as a redox reaction.

parts.content: State one reason why electrolysis of molten salts is more energy-intensive than electrolysis of aqueous solutions.
parts.markscheme:
- State the reason: high temperature/energy is required to melt the ionic compound or to overcome strong electrostatic forces in the lattice <marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

steps.title: Analyze the starting states
steps.content:
To understand why one process uses more energy, we first look at the state of the substance. 

*   **Aqueous solutions:** The salt is already dissolved in water, meaning it is in a liquid-like state at room temperature.
*   **Molten salts:** The substance starts as a solid (an ionic lattice) and must be converted into a liquid to allow ions to move and conduct electricity.

steps.title: Overcoming the ionic lattice
steps.content:
Ionic compounds like $MgCl_2$ are held together by very strong **electrostatic forces** in a giant lattice structure. 

Breaking these bonds to melt the solid requires a significant input of thermal energy. In contrast, aqueous solutions are already in the liquid phase and do not require this initial energy for a phase change.

steps.title: Maintaining high temperatures
steps.content:
Using the **Thermal Requirement Analysis**, we see that molten electrolysis operates at extremely high temperatures (for magnesium chloride, this is above $714^\circ\text{C}$).

Energy is continuously required to:
1. Reach the melting point.
2. Maintain the electrolyte in a liquid state throughout the industrial process.

Because aqueous electrolysis happens at or near room temperature, it avoids these massive thermal energy requirements.

highlights.text: high temperature/energy is required to melt the ionic compound or to overcome strong electrostatic forces in the lattice

steps.title: Final Conclusion
steps.content: Therefore, the reason it is more energy-intensive is that **high energy/temperature is required to melt the salt** and overcome the strong forces holding the lattice together.

steps.title: Compare the starting states
steps.content: To understand why electrolysis of molten salts is energy-intensive, we first look at the difference between an aqueous solution and a molten salt. In an aqueous solution, the ionic compound is already dissolved in water, meaning the ions are already separated and mobile at room temperature. In contrast, to get a molten salt, we must start with the solid compound.

steps.title: Analyze the ionic lattice
steps.content:
In the solid state, $MgCl_2$ exists as a giant ionic lattice. This structure is held together by **strong electrostatic forces of attraction** between the oppositely charged ions ($Mg^{2+}$ and $Cl^{-}$). 

This is a key concept from **Topic S2.1.3: Lattice structures and properties**. Because these forces are so strong, a significant amount of thermal energy is required to vibrate the ions enough to break the lattice structure and allow the salt to melt.

steps.title: Evaluate energy consumption
steps.content: The energy-intensive nature of this process comes from the high temperatures needed to reach the melting point. While aqueous electrolysis happens at or near room temperature, molten electrolysis requires constant heating to overcome the lattice energy and keep the substance in a liquid state so that the ions can move to the electrodes.

steps.title: State the final reason
steps.content:
According to the markscheme, the primary reason is that a high amount of energy (heat) is required to melt the ionic compound or to overcome the strong electrostatic forces within the lattice structure.

Final Answer: **High temperature/energy is required to melt the ionic compound and overcome the strong electrostatic forces in the lattice.**

highlights.text: high temperature/energy is required to melt the ionic compound or to overcome strong electrostatic forces in the lattice

parts.content: State the oxidation number of chlorine in $\mathrm{Cl}_2$.
parts.markscheme:
- $0$ <marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

steps.title: Identify the chemical species
steps.content:
The question asks for the oxidation number of chlorine in the molecule $\mathrm{Cl}_2$. First, we look at the provided redox reaction and locate the species:

$$\mathrm{Cl}_2(\mathrm{aq}) + 2\mathrm{I}^-(\mathrm{aq}) \rightarrow 2\mathrm{Cl}^-(\mathrm{aq}) + \mathrm{I}_2(\mathrm{aq})$$

Here, $\mathrm{Cl}_2$ is a reactant.

highlights.text: State the oxidation number of chlorine in $\mathrm{Cl}_2$.

steps.title: Classify as a pure element
steps.content:
Next, we determine if the substance is a compound or a pure element. A pure element consists of only one type of atom and is not combined with any other different elements. 

In $\mathrm{Cl}_2$, there are two chlorine atoms bonded together, but no other elements are present. Therefore, $\mathrm{Cl}_2$ is a **pure element** in its molecular form.

steps.title: Apply Rule for Pure Elements
steps.content:
According to the fundamental rules for assigning oxidation states (found in **Topic R3.2: Electron transfer reactions**), the oxidation number of any element in its pure, uncombined state is always **zero**. 

This applies regardless of whether the element exists as single atoms (like $\mathrm{Ar}$) or as diatomic molecules (like $\mathrm{Cl}_2$ or $\mathrm{I}_2$).

$$\text{Oxidation number of Cl in } \mathrm{Cl}_2 = 0$$

steps.title: Identify the chemical species
steps.content: To find the oxidation number of chlorine in this reaction, we first look at the reactant $\mathrm{Cl}_2(\mathrm{aq})$. This is a diatomic molecule consisting of two chlorine atoms.

steps.title: Apply Neutral Molecule Summation
steps.content:
A fundamental rule in redox chemistry (covered in **Topic R3.2: Electron transfer reactions**) is that the sum of oxidation states for all atoms in a neutral molecule must equal zero. 

Let $x$ represent the oxidation number of a single chlorine atom. Since there are two chlorine atoms in $\mathrm{Cl}_2$, we set up the following equation:

$$2x = 0$$

steps.title: Solve for x
steps.content:
To find the oxidation number of one chlorine atom, we solve for $x$ by dividing both sides of the equation by 2.

$$\begin{aligned} 2x &= 0 \\ x &= \frac{0}{2} \\ x &= 0 \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Input 0 / 2 into the calculation screen.

ti84.instructions: Enter 0 / 2 and press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Type 0 / 2 in the expression bar.

calculator.description: Verify the division to find the oxidation state.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The oxidation number of chlorine in $\mathrm{Cl}_2$ is $0$. 

This follows the general rule that the oxidation state of any element in its standard state is always zero.

parts.content: Identify the species that is reduced.
parts.markscheme:
- Chlorine / $\mathrm{Cl}_2$ <marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

steps.title: Define reduction
steps.content:
In chemistry, **reduction** is defined as the process where an atom or ion undergoes a **decrease in oxidation state**. To identify which species is reduced in this reaction, we will analyze the oxidation states of the chlorine atoms before and after the reaction.

This concept is a core part of **Topic R3.2: Electron transfer reactions**.

highlights.text: Identify the species that is reduced.

steps.title: Oxidation state of chlorine (reactant)
steps.content:
First, we look at the reactant $\mathrm{Cl}_2(\mathrm{aq})$. 

According to the rules for assigning oxidation states, any element in its natural, uncombined state has an oxidation state of $0$. Therefore, the oxidation state of chlorine in $\mathrm{Cl}_2$ is $0$.

steps.title: Oxidation state of chlorine (product)
steps.content:
Next, we examine the product $\mathrm{Cl}^-(\mathrm{aq})$. 

For a monoatomic ion, the oxidation state is always equal to its ionic charge. Since the chloride ion has a charge of $1-$, its oxidation state is $-1$.

steps.title: Analyze the change
steps.content:
Now, let's look at how the oxidation state of chlorine changed during the reaction:

$$\text{Initial (in } \mathrm{Cl}_2\text{)} = 0$$
$$\text{Final (in } \mathrm{Cl}^-\text{)} = -1$$

Because the oxidation state decreased from $0$ to $-1$, we can conclude that the chlorine atoms have been reduced.

gdc.instructions: Calculate the difference between the final and initial states.

ti84.instructions: Calculate the difference between the final and initial states.

desmos.instructions: Calculate the difference between the final and initial states.

calculator.description: Verify the change in oxidation state.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The species that is reduced is the one that contains the atom undergoing a decrease in oxidation state. 

Therefore, the species that is reduced is **chlorine** (or $\mathrm{Cl}_2$).

steps.title: Deconstruct into half-equations
steps.content:
To find out which species is reduced, we first need to break the overall equation into two half-equations. This allows us to see exactly where the electrons are moving. 

The overall reaction is:
$$\mathrm{Cl}_2(\mathrm{aq}) + 2\mathrm{I}^-(\mathrm{aq}) \rightarrow 2\mathrm{Cl}^-(\mathrm{aq}) + \mathrm{I}_2(\mathrm{aq})$$

We look at the change for chlorine and the change for iodine separately.

steps.title: Analyze the chlorine species
steps.content:
Let's look at what happens to the chlorine. It starts as $\mathrm{Cl}_2$ and ends up as $2\mathrm{Cl}^-$. 

To balance the charge, we must add electrons to the reactant side:
$$\mathrm{Cl}_2(\mathrm{aq}) + 2\mathrm{e}^- \rightarrow 2\mathrm{Cl}^-(\mathrm{aq})$$

This half-equation shows that chlorine is **gaining** electrons.

steps.title: Apply the OIL RIG principle
steps.content:
In IB Chemistry, we use the mnemonic **OIL RIG** to remember the definitions of oxidation and reduction:
- **O**xidation **I**s **L**oss (of electrons)
- **R**eduction **I**s **G**ain (of electrons)

Since the chlorine molecules ($ \mathrm{Cl}_2 $) are gaining electrons to become chloride ions ($ \mathrm{Cl}^- $), chlorine is the species being reduced. 

This concept is a core part of **Topic R3.2: Electron transfer reactions** in your syllabus.

steps.title: Identify the reduced species
steps.content:
The question asks for the species that is reduced. Based on our half-equation analysis, that species is chlorine.

Final Answer: **Chlorine** or **$\mathrm{Cl}_2$**

parts.content: Explain why this reaction is classified as redox.
parts.markscheme:
- Chlorine gains electrons (is reduced) <marks>R1</marks>
- Iodide / $\mathrm{I}^-$ loses electrons (is oxidized) <marks>R1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Define redox via electron transfer
steps.content:
In chemistry, the term **redox** is a combination of **red**uction and **ox**idation. Using the electron transfer approach, we use the mnemonic **OIL RIG**:
- **O**xidation **I**s **L**oss of electrons.
- **R**eduction **I**s **G**ain of electrons.

A reaction is classified as redox if electrons are transferred from one species to another.

steps.title: Analyze chlorine's electron change
steps.content:
Let's look at what happens to the chlorine molecule ($$\mathrm{Cl}_2$$). In the reaction, it is converted into chloride ions ($$\mathrm{Cl}^-$$):

$$\mathrm{Cl}_2(\mathrm{aq}) + 2e^- \rightarrow 2\mathrm{Cl}^-(\mathrm{aq})$$

Because the chlorine molecule **gains electrons**, it undergoes **reduction**.

steps.title: Analyze iodide's electron change
steps.content:
Now, let's look at the iodide ions ($$\mathrm{I}^-$$). They are converted into iodine molecules ($$\mathrm{I}_2$$):

$$2\mathrm{I}^-(\mathrm{aq}) \rightarrow \mathrm{I}_2(\mathrm{aq}) + 2e^-$$

Because the iodide ions **lose electrons**, they undergo **oxidation**.

steps.title: Conclude redox classification
steps.content:
Since the reaction involves both the **loss of electrons** (by $$\mathrm{I}^-$$) and the **gain of electrons** (by $$\mathrm{Cl}_2$$), it is a redox reaction. 

To earn full marks on an IB exam, you must clearly state both processes:
1.  **Chlorine gains electrons** (reduction).
2.  **Iodide loses electrons** (oxidation).

steps.title: Define Redox with Oxidation States
steps.content:
A **redox** reaction is characterized by a change in the oxidation states of the elements involved. This involves two simultaneous processes: **oxidation** (an increase in oxidation state) and **reduction** (a decrease in oxidation state). 

This concept is a core part of **Reactivity 3.2: Electron transfer reactions**. To explain why this specific reaction is redox, we need to determine the oxidation state for each species before and after the reaction.

steps.title: Analyze Chlorine's Oxidation State
steps.content:
First, let's look at chlorine:

1. In the reactants, $\mathrm{Cl}_2$ is an element in its standard state, so its oxidation state is **0**.
2. In the products, $\mathrm{Cl}^-$ is a monoatomic ion, so its oxidation state is equal to its charge, which is **-1**.

Since the oxidation state decreases from $0$ to $-1$, chlorine undergoes **reduction**.

gdc.instructions: Subtract the initial state from the final state.

ti84.instructions: Subtract the initial state from the final state.

desmos.instructions: Subtract the initial state from the final state.

calculator.description: Calculate the change in oxidation state for Chlorine.

steps.title: Analyze Iodine's Oxidation State
steps.content:
Next, let's look at iodine:

1. In the reactants, $\mathrm{I}^-$ is a monoatomic ion, so its oxidation state is **-1**.
2. In the products, $\mathrm{I}_2$ is an element in its standard state, so its oxidation state is **0**.

Since the oxidation state increases from $-1$ to $0$, iodine undergoes **oxidation**.

steps.title: Conclude the Redox Classification
steps.content:
The reaction is classified as **redox** because both oxidation and reduction occur simultaneously, as evidenced by the change in oxidation states:

- Chlorine is reduced (state decreases from $0$ to $-1$).
- Iodide is oxidized (state increases from $-1$ to $0$).

In an IB exam, identifying that one species is reduced and the other is oxidized earns the 2 marks available.

steps.title: Define the redox process
steps.content: To explain why a reaction is classified as **redox**, we need to show that both **reduction** and **oxidation** are taking place. This is best done by splitting the full chemical equation into two half-equations to track the movement of electrons.

steps.title: Analyze Chlorine reduction
steps.content:
First, we look at the chlorine species. The neutral chlorine molecule ($\mathrm{Cl}_2$) is converted into chloride ions ($2\mathrm{Cl}^-$). For this to happen, the chlorine must gain two electrons:

$$\mathrm{Cl}_2(\mathrm{aq}) + 2\mathrm{e}^- \rightarrow 2\mathrm{Cl}^-(\mathrm{aq})$$

According to the **OIL RIG** acronym (Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain), because **chlorine gains electrons**, it is undergoing **reduction**.

steps.title: Analyze Iodide oxidation
steps.content:
Next, we look at the iodide ions. Two iodide ions ($2\mathrm{I}^-$) are converted into a neutral iodine molecule ($\mathrm{I}_2$). To lose their negative charge, the iodide ions must release electrons:

$$2\mathrm{I}^-(\mathrm{aq}) \rightarrow \mathrm{I}_2(\mathrm{aq}) + 2\mathrm{e}^-$$

Because the **iodide ions lose electrons**, they are undergoing **oxidation**.

steps.title: Classify as redox
steps.content:
A reaction is classified as **redox** when there is a simultaneous transfer of electrons from one species to another. 

Since we have demonstrated that chlorine is reduced (gains electrons) and iodide is oxidized (loses electrons), the reaction is a redox reaction. This meets the two criteria for the marks:
1. Chlorine gains electrons (is reduced).
2. Iodide loses electrons (is oxidized).

parts.content: State the meaning of the term voltaic cell.
parts.markscheme: A voltaic cell is a device that converts chemical energy into electrical energy through a spontaneous redox reaction. <marks>1</marks>

steps.title: Identify the energy transformation
steps.content:
To define a **voltaic cell** using a chemical approach, we first look at what it does with energy. In these cells, a chemical process is used to generate an electric current. Therefore, the primary function is to convert **chemical energy** into **electrical energy**.

This is the opposite of an electrolytic cell, which uses electrical energy to drive a chemical change.

steps.title: Define the reaction type
steps.content:
The second part of the definition involves the nature of the chemical reaction. For a cell to produce electricity without an external power source, the reaction must be:
1.  **Redox**: It must involve the transfer of electrons (oxidation and reduction).
2.  **Spontaneous**: The reaction happens naturally on its own ($$\Delta G < 0$$).

This concept is covered in **Topic R3.2: Electron transfer reactions** of your syllabus.

steps.title: Synthesize the final definition
steps.content:
By combining the energy transformation and the reaction type, we get the standard IB definition required for the mark:

**A voltaic cell is a device that converts chemical energy into electrical energy through a spontaneous redox reaction.**

parts.content: Explain the purpose of the salt bridge in the setup.
parts.markscheme:
The salt bridge allows ions to move between the two half-cells to maintain electrical neutrality. <marks>1</marks>

It completes the circuit without mixing the different solutions. <marks>1</marks>

steps.title: Identify the internal connection
steps.content:
In a voltaic cell, the **salt bridge** (labeled as part 3) is the physical link between the two half-cells. Its first major role is to **complete the electrical circuit**. 

While electrons flow through the external wire (the external circuit), a complete loop is needed for current to flow. The salt bridge provides an 'internal' path that allows charge to move between the solutions without actually mixing the two different electrolytes.

steps.title: Maintain electrical neutrality
steps.content:
The second and most critical role is **maintaining electrical neutrality** through the migration of ions. 

As the cell operates:
1. At the **anode**, oxidation occurs, creating an excess of positive ions (e.g., $Zn^{2+}$) in the solution.
2. At the **cathode**, reduction occurs, removing positive ions (e.g., $Cu^{2+}$) from the solution, leaving it with a net negative charge.

Without the salt bridge, this charge build-up would immediately stop the flow of electrons. The salt bridge contains an inert electrolyte (like $KNO_3$) whose ions migrate into the half-cells to balance these charges: **anions** move toward the anode and **cations** move toward the cathode.

steps.title: Summary of marks
steps.content:
To earn the full 2 marks on an IB exam, your explanation must cover both the physical and chemical functions:

*   **Mark 1:** State that it **completes the circuit** (without mixing the solutions).
*   **Mark 2:** State that it allows **ions to move** to **maintain electrical neutrality** (or balance the charge).

highlights.text: completes the circuit without mixing the different solutions.
allows ions to move between the two half-cells to maintain electrical neutrality.

parts.content: Annotate parts 1-6 in the diagram for a voltaic cell made from a zinc electrode in $\text{Zn(NO}_3\text{)}_2\text{(aq)}$ and a copper electrode in $\text{Cu(NO}_3\text{)}_2\text{(aq)}$.
parts.markscheme:
1: $\text{Zn(NO}_3\text{)}_2\text{(aq)}$ (zinc nitrate solution) 

2: $\text{Zn(s)}$ (zinc electrode) 

3: Voltmeter 

4: $\text{Cu(s)}$ (copper electrode) 

5: $\text{Cu(NO}_3\text{)}_2\text{(aq)}$ (copper(II) nitrate solution) 

6: Salt bridge (e.g., $\text{KNO}_3$ or $\text{NaNO}_3$) 

Award [1] for every two correct identifications. <marks>3</marks>

steps.title: Analyze the voltaic cell
steps.content:
To solve this, we will use **Structural Role Identification**. This means we look at where each part is located in the diagram to determine its function in the voltaic cell. 

A voltaic cell converts chemical energy into electrical energy using two half-cells. Each half-cell consists of a solid electrode and a liquid electrolyte solution. This topic is covered in **Topic R3.2: Electron transfer reactions**.

highlights.text: zinc electrode in Zn(NO3)2(aq) and a copper electrode in Cu(NO3)2(aq)

steps.title: Identify the solid electrodes
steps.content:
Parts **2** and **4** are the solid rods submerged in the liquids. These are the **electrodes**. 

In this cell, the electrodes are made of zinc and copper metals. By convention, the more reactive metal (zinc) is often placed on the left, but we simply need to pair the metal with its corresponding solution.

- **2**: $\text{Zn(s)}$ (Zinc electrode)
- **4**: $\text{Cu(s)}$ (Copper electrode)

steps.title: Identify the liquid electrolytes
steps.content:
Parts **1** and **5** represent the aqueous solutions in the beakers. These are the **electrolytes**, which must contain the ions of the metal electrodes to allow the half-reactions to occur.

- Since electrode 2 is zinc, solution **1** is $\text{Zn(NO}_3\text{)}_2\text{(aq)}$.
- Since electrode 4 is copper, solution **5** is $\text{Cu(NO}_3\text{)}_2\text{(aq)}$.

steps.title: Identify the external circuit
steps.content:
Part **3** is the device connected to the wires between the two electrodes. Its role is to measure the electrical potential difference (voltage) produced by the cell.

- **3**: **Voltmeter**

steps.title: Identify the salt bridge
steps.content:
Part **6** is the inverted U-tube that connects the two separate beakers. Its role is to allow the migration of ions to maintain charge neutrality in both half-cells as the reaction proceeds.

- **6**: **Salt bridge** (typically containing an inert electrolyte like $\text{KNO}_3$ or $\text{NaNO}_3$)

steps.title: Summary of annotations
steps.content:
Based on the structural roles identified, here are the full annotations for the diagram:

1: $\text{Zn(NO}_3\text{)}_2\text{(aq)}$ 
2: $\text{Zn(s)}$ 
3: Voltmeter 
4: $\text{Cu(s)}$ 
5: $\text{Cu(NO}_3\text{)}_2\text{(aq)}$ 
6: Salt bridge

According to the markscheme, you earn **1 mark for every two correct identifications**, for a total of **3 marks**.

steps.title: Determine relative reactivity
steps.content:
To assign the parts of the cell, we first compare the reactivity of the two metals provided: Zinc ($Zn$) and Copper ($Cu$). 

According to the activity series (covered in **Topic R3.2: Electron transfer reactions**), Zinc is more reactive than Copper. This means Zinc has a greater tendency to lose electrons and undergo oxidation.

steps.title: Define Anode and Cathode
steps.content:
In a voltaic cell, the more reactive metal acts as the **anode** (where oxidation occurs), and the less reactive metal acts as the **cathode** (where reduction occurs).

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Anode (Oxidation):} & \quad \text{Zn(s)} \rightarrow \text{Zn}^{2+}\text{(aq)} + 2\text{e}^- \\ \text{Cathode (Reduction):} & \quad \text{Cu}^{2+}\text{(aq)} + 2\text{e}^- \rightarrow \text{Cu(s)} \end{aligned}$$

By convention, the anode is typically drawn on the left and the cathode on the right.

steps.title: Identify electrodes (2 and 4)
steps.content:
Since Zinc is the anode and Copper is the cathode, we can identify the metal strips (the electrodes):

*   **Part 2** is the Zinc electrode: $\text{Zn(s)}$
*   **Part 4** is the Copper electrode: $\text{Cu(s)}$

steps.title: Identify solutions (1 and 5)
steps.content:
Each half-cell consists of a metal electrode immersed in a solution of its own ions. 

*   The Zinc electrode (**2**) must be in the Zinc solution: **Part 1** is $\text{Zn(NO}_3\text{)}_2\text{(aq)}$.
*   The Copper electrode (**4**) must be in the Copper solution: **Part 5** is $\text{Cu(NO}_3\text{)}_2\text{(aq)}$.

steps.title: Identify apparatus (3 and 6)
steps.content:
Finally, we identify the remaining components of the circuit:

*   **Part 3** is the **voltmeter**, which is connected in the external circuit to measure the potential difference (voltage) of the cell.
*   **Part 6** is the **salt bridge**, which connects the two half-cells and allows for the migration of ions to maintain electrical neutrality.

parts.content: Write the half-equations for the reactions at the anode and cathode.
parts.markscheme:
Anode (oxidation): $\text{Zn(s)} \rightarrow \text{Zn}^{2+}\text{(aq)} + 2\text{e}^-$ <marks>1</marks>

Cathode (reduction): $\text{Cu}^{2+}\text{(aq)} + 2\text{e}^- \rightarrow \text{Cu(s)}$ <marks>1</marks>

steps.title: Identify electrode potentials
steps.content:
To determine what happens at each electrode, we look up the **Standard Electrode Potentials ($E^{\ominus}$)** in the IB Data Booklet for the two metals present in the cell: zinc ($Zn$) and copper ($Cu$).

These values are typically given for reduction reactions:

$$Zn^{2+}(aq) + 2e^- \rightleftharpoons Zn(s) \quad E^{\ominus} = -0.76 \text{ V}$$
$$Cu^{2+}(aq) + 2e^- \rightleftharpoons Cu(s) \quad E^{\ominus} = +0.34 \text{ V}$$

steps.title: Compare potentials for reactivity
steps.content:
In a voltaic cell, the half-cell with the **more negative** (or less positive) $E^{\ominus}$ value has a greater tendency to lose electrons. This indicates the site of **oxidation**, which is the **anode**.

Since $-0.76 \text{ V}$ is more negative than $+0.34 \text{ V}$, zinc will be oxidized and copper will be reduced.

steps.title: Write the anode equation
steps.content:
At the **anode**, oxidation occurs. This means the solid zinc metal ($Zn$) loses two electrons to become zinc ions ($Zn^{2+}$). 

$$\text{Anode (oxidation): } Zn(s) \rightarrow Zn^{2+}(aq) + 2e^-$$

steps.title: Write the cathode equation
steps.content:
At the **cathode**, reduction occurs. The copper ions ($Cu^{2+}$) in the solution gain two electrons to form solid copper metal ($Cu$).

$$\text{Cathode (reduction): } Cu^{2+}(aq) + 2e^- \rightarrow Cu(s)$$ 

You get 1 mark for each correctly written half-equation!

steps.title: Compare reactivity using Activity Series
steps.content:
To determine which reaction happens where, we first look at the **Activity Series**. In a voltaic cell, the more reactive metal will lose electrons (oxidation) more easily. 

Zinc ($Zn$) is higher in the activity series than copper ($Cu$), making it more reactive. This means the zinc electrode will be the site of oxidation, which is the **anode**.

steps.title: Write the anode half-equation
steps.content:
Since oxidation occurs at the anode, zinc atoms from the electrode lose two electrons to form zinc ions in the solution. This is covered in **Topic R3.2: Electron transfer reactions**.

$$\text{Zn(s)} \rightarrow \text{Zn}^{2+}\text{(aq)} + 2\text{e}^-$$

steps.title: Write the cathode half-equation
steps.content:
The electrons released by the zinc travel through the external circuit to the copper electrode, which is the **cathode**. At the cathode, reduction occurs. Copper ions in the solution gain these electrons to form solid copper atoms.

$$\text{Cu}^{2+}\text{(aq)} + 2\text{e}^- \rightarrow \text{Cu(s)}$$

steps.title: Final check of equations
steps.content:
Ensure that both half-equations are balanced for both mass and charge, and include state symbols for full marks as shown in the markscheme:

**Anode (oxidation):**
$$\text{Zn(s)} \rightarrow \text{Zn}^{2+}\text{(aq)} + 2\text{e}^-$$

**Cathode (reduction):**
$$\text{Cu}^{2+}\text{(aq)} + 2\text{e}^- \rightarrow \text{Cu(s)}$$

options.content: $+0.60\text{ V}$; electrons flow from Sn to Cr
options.markscheme: $E^\circ_{\text{cell}}=E^\circ_{\text{cathode}}-E^\circ_{\text{anode}}=(-0.14)-(-0.74)=+0.60\text{ V}$. Sn has the more positive reduction potential so it is the cathode; electrons therefore flow from Cr (anode) to Sn (cathode), so this direction is incorrect.

options.content: $+0.60\text{ V}$; electrons flow from Cr to Sn
options.markscheme: Sn is the cathode because $E^\circ(\text{Sn}^{2+}/\text{Sn})=-0.14\text{ V}$ is more positive than $E^\circ(\text{Cr}^{3+}/\text{Cr})=-0.74\text{ V}$. Thus $E^\circ_{\text{cell}}=(-0.14)-(-0.74)=+0.60\text{ V}$ and electrons flow from Cr (anode) to Sn (cathode).

options.content: $-0.60\text{ V}$; electrons flow from Cr to Sn
options.markscheme: With electrons flowing from Cr to Sn, Sn is the cathode and Cr is the anode, giving $E^\circ_{\text{cell}}=(-0.14)-(-0.74)=+0.60\text{ V}$, not $-0.60\text{ V}$.

options.content: $+0.74\text{ V}$; electrons flow from Cr to Sn
options.markscheme: The cell potential is found by subtracting the anode reduction potential from the cathode reduction potential: $(-0.14)-(-0.74)=+0.60\text{ V}$. $+0.74\text{ V}$ is not the correct difference for these half-cells.

[1 mark]

steps.title: Identify the concept
steps.content: This question asks for a fundamental definition in redox chemistry. In the IB curriculum (Topic R3.2.1), we classify chemical reactions based on the transfer of electrons between species.

steps.title: Define oxidation
steps.content:
By direct definition, **oxidation** is the **loss of electrons** from a substance.

When a species undergoes oxidation, its oxidation state increases because it is losing negatively charged particles (electrons).

highlights.text: Define the term oxidation in terms of electrons.

steps.title: Use a mnemonic
steps.content:
A very common and helpful way to remember the definitions of oxidation and reduction is the acronym **OIL RIG**:

- **O**xidation **I**s **L**oss (of electrons)
- **R**eduction **I**s **G**ain (of electrons)

steps.title: Final Answer
steps.content: To secure the mark, you must state that oxidation is the loss of electrons.

steps.title: Identify oxidation state rules
steps.content:
To solve this using algebra, we first recall two fundamental rules for oxidation states:
1. The oxidation state of Hydrogen ($H$) is usually $+1$ when bonded to non-metals.
2. The sum of the oxidation states of all atoms in a neutral molecule, like $\text{H}_2\text{O}$, must equal $0$.

These concepts are covered in **Topic S3.1.6 Oxidation states**.

steps.title: Formulate the algebraic equation
steps.content:
Let the oxidation state of oxygen be $x$. Since there are two hydrogen atoms and one oxygen atom in $\text{H}_2\text{O}$, we can set up the following equation:

$$2(\text{oxidation state of H}) + 1(\text{oxidation state of O}) = 0$$

Substituting the known value for hydrogen ($+1$):

$$2(+1) + x = 0$$

steps.title: Solve for oxygen
steps.content:
Now, we solve the algebraic equation for $x$:

$$\begin{aligned} 2 + x &= 0 \\ x &= -2 \end{aligned}$$

This means the oxidation number of oxygen in water is $-2$.

gdc.instructions: Enter the expression into the calculation menu.

ti84.instructions: Enter the expression into the home screen.

desmos.instructions: Type the expression to check the result.

calculator.description: Verify the sum of oxidation states equals zero.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content: Based on our algebraic calculation, the oxidation number of oxygen in $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ is $-2$.

steps.title: Identify the standard rule
steps.content: To find the oxidation number of oxygen in $\text{H}_2\text{O}$, we apply the standard rules for assigning oxidation states. In IB Chemistry, the most common rule for oxygen is that it has an oxidation number of $-2$ in almost all of its compounds.

highlights.text: oxidation number of oxygen in H2O

steps.title: Check for exceptions
steps.content:
There are only two main exceptions to the $-2$ rule for oxygen:
1. **Peroxides**: Such as $\text{H}_2\text{O}_2$, where oxygen is $-1$.
2. **Fluorides**: Such as $\text{OF}_2$, where oxygen has a positive oxidation state because fluorine is more electronegative.

Since $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ is a standard oxide and not a peroxide or a fluoride, these exceptions do not apply.

steps.title: Verify using hydrogen
steps.content:
We can verify this by looking at the total charge of the molecule. The sum of oxidation numbers in a neutral compound like $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ must be $0$.

Hydrogen ($H$) has an oxidation number of $+1$ in compounds with non-metals. In $\text{H}_2\text{O}$, we have two hydrogen atoms:

$$\begin{aligned} 2(H) + 1(O) &= 0 \\ 2(+1) + x &= 0 \\ 2 + x &= 0 \\ x &= -2 \end{aligned}$$

This confirms our standard rule.

gdc.instructions: Solve the linear equation 2 + x = 0 for x.

ti84.instructions: Solve for x in the equation 2(1) + x = 0.

desmos.instructions: Graph the equation y = 2 + x and find the x-intercept (where y = 0).

calculator.description: Calculate the oxidation number of oxygen by setting the sum of oxidation states to zero.

steps.title: State the final value
steps.content: The oxidation number of oxygen in $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ is $-2$. This corresponds to the markscheme answer and follows the fundamental rules of redox chemistry found in **Topic R3.2: Electron transfer reactions**.

parts.content:
Deduce which species is oxidized in the reaction:
$\text{Mg}(s) + \text{Cl}_2(g) \rightarrow \text{MgCl}_2(s)$
parts.markscheme:
* $\text{Mg}$ / magnesium **AND** oxidation number increases from $0$ to $+2$ <marks>A1</marks>

[1 mark]

steps.title: Assign reactant oxidation numbers
steps.content:
To find out what is being oxidized, we first assign oxidation numbers to the reactants. A fundamental rule in redox chemistry is that any element in its uncombined, standard state has an oxidation number of $0$.

In the equation:
$$\text{Mg}(s) + \text{Cl}_2(g) \rightarrow \text{MgCl}_2(s)$$

Both $\text{Mg}$ and $\text{Cl}_2$ are elements, so we assign them both an oxidation number of $0$.

steps.title: Assign product oxidation numbers
steps.content:
Now we look at the product, $\text{MgCl}_2$. This is an ionic compound. For simple ions in a compound, the oxidation number is equal to the charge of the ion.

*   **Magnesium (Mg)** is in Group 2 of the periodic table, so it forms a $2+$ ion. Its oxidation number is **$+2$**.
*   **Chlorine (Cl)** is in Group 17, so it forms a $1-$ ion. Its oxidation number is **$-1$**.

You can review these trends in **Topic S3.1: The periodic table: Classification of elements** if you need a refresher on group charges!

steps.title: Identify the oxidized species
steps.content:
Finally, we compare the changes. **Oxidation** is defined as an **increase** in oxidation number.

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Initial Mg} &= 0 \\ \text{Final Mg} &= +2 \end{aligned}$$

Since the oxidation number of magnesium increases from $0$ to $+2$, magnesium ($ \text{Mg} $) is the species that is oxidized.

steps.title: Final Conclusion
steps.content:
To get the mark in an IB exam, you must clearly identify the species and state the numerical change.

**Answer:** $\text{Mg}$ is oxidized because its oxidation number increases from $0$ to $+2$.

steps.title: Recall the OIL RIG mnemonic
steps.content:
To solve this using the electron transfer approach, we use the mnemonic **OIL RIG**:

*   **O**xidation **I**s **L**oss (of electrons)
*   **R**eduction **I**s **G**ain (of electrons)

We need to find which species is losing electrons in the reaction.

steps.title: Identify the species' states
steps.content:
Let's look at the reaction provided:

$$\text{Mg}(s) + \text{Cl}_2(g) \rightarrow \text{MgCl}_2(s)$$

1.  On the reactant side, magnesium is a neutral metal atom: $\text{Mg}^0$.
2.  On the product side, $\text{MgCl}_2$ is an ionic compound. Magnesium exists as the magnesium ion, $\text{Mg}^{2+}$.

steps.title: Observe electron loss
steps.content:
To turn a neutral magnesium atom into a $2+$ ion, it must lose electrons. We can represent this with a half-equation:

$$\text{Mg} \rightarrow \text{Mg}^{2+} + 2e^-$$

Because magnesium has lost two electrons, its oxidation number increases from $0$ to $+2$.

gdc.instructions: Subtract the initial state from the final state.

ti84.instructions: Subtract the initial state from the final state.

desmos.instructions: Subtract the initial state from the final state.

calculator.description: Calculate the change in oxidation state from 0 to +2.

steps.title: State the oxidized species
steps.content:
Applying our **OIL RIG** rule, since magnesium metal is the species that lost electrons, it is the species that has been **oxidized**.

Final Answer: **Mg** (or magnesium).
