parts.content: State the half-equation that defines the hydrogen half-cell.
parts.markscheme:
- $2\text{H}^+(\text{aq}) + 2\text{e}^- \rightleftharpoons \text{H}_2(\text{g})$

**OR**

- $\text{H}^+(\text{aq}) + \text{e}^- \rightleftharpoons \frac{1}{2}\text{H}_2(\text{g})$
<marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Identify the reference electrode
steps.content: In electrochemistry, we need a common baseline to compare the strengths of different oxidizing and reducing agents. The hydrogen half-cell, known as the **Standard Hydrogen Electrode (SHE)**, is the universal reference point used for this purpose.

steps.title: Locate the reference potential
steps.content: To find the specific equation, open your IB Data Booklet to the table of **Standard reduction potentials**. Since the hydrogen half-cell is the reference, its standard electrode potential ($E^{\ominus}$) is defined as exactly $0.00\text{V}$. Scan the voltage column until you find this value.

steps.title: State the half-equation
steps.content:
The entry corresponding to $0.00\text{V}$ shows the reduction of hydrogen ions to hydrogen gas. In IB exams, you can write this using whole numbers or fractions:

$$\begin{aligned} 2\text{H}^+(\text{aq}) + 2\text{e}^- &\rightleftharpoons \text{H}_2(\text{g}) \\ &\text{OR} \\ \text{H}^+(\text{aq}) + \text{e}^- &\rightleftharpoons \frac{1}{2}\text{H}_2(\text{g}) \end{aligned}$$

Remember to include the equilibrium sign ($\rightleftharpoons$) as standard electrode potentials are defined for systems at equilibrium.

steps.title: Identify the chemical species
steps.content:
To define the hydrogen half-cell (also known as the Standard Hydrogen Electrode or SHE), we first identify the two forms of hydrogen that exist in equilibrium. This electrode uses hydrogen ions in an aqueous solution and hydrogen gas.

*   **Aqueous phase**: $\text{H}^+(\text{aq})$ ions at a concentration of $1.0 \text{ mol dm}^{-3}$.
*   **Gas phase**: $\text{H}_2(\text{g})$ at a pressure of $100 \text{ kPa}$.

This concept is a core part of **Topic R3.2: Electron transfer reactions**, where we learn that all standard electrode potentials are measured relative to this specific cell.

highlights.text: State the half-equation that defines the hydrogen half-cell.

steps.title: Recall the balanced half-equation
steps.content:
In IB Chemistry, electrode potentials are defined using reduction half-equations. This means we show the species gaining electrons on the left side. 

Because we are describing an electrode potential, we use the equilibrium sign ($\rightleftharpoons$) to show the process is reversible. You can write the balanced equation in two ways depending on whether you prefer whole numbers or fractions:

**Option 1 (Whole numbers):**
$$2\text{H}^+(\text{aq}) + 2\text{e}^- \rightleftharpoons \text{H}_2(\text{g})$$

**Option 2 (Per mole of electrons):**
$$\text{H}^+(\text{aq}) + \text{e}^- \rightleftharpoons \frac{1}{2}\text{H}_2(\text{g})$$

Both forms are correct and will earn the mark. Remember to include the state symbols to show the transition from aqueous ions to gaseous molecules.

parts.content: Explain why the hydrogen half-cell is assigned a potential of $0.00\text{ V}$.
parts.markscheme:
- The hydrogen half-cell is used as the universal reference and is defined as $0.00\text{ V}$ by convention.
<marks>1 mark</marks>
- All electrode potentials are measured relative to this reference.
<marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Identify the measurement problem
steps.content:
In electrochemistry, it is impossible to measure the **absolute** electrical potential of a single half-cell in isolation. We can only measure the **potential difference** (voltage) between two different half-cells when they are connected to form a complete circuit.

This concept is a key part of **Topic R3.2: Electron transfer reactions** in your syllabus.

steps.title: Define an arbitrary zero
steps.content: To create a consistent scale for comparison, chemists needed a starting point. By international convention, the hydrogen half-cell (the Standard Hydrogen Electrode, or SHE) was chosen as the universal reference. Its potential is **arbitrarily assigned** a value of exactly $0.00\text{ V}$ at all temperatures.

steps.title: Enable relative comparisons
steps.content: By setting this reference point to zero, all other standard electrode potentials can be measured **relative** to it. This allows us to rank substances by their strength as oxidizing or reducing agents, as seen in the Standard Electrode Potential tables in your data booklet.

steps.title: Final Summary for Marks
steps.content:
To secure full marks on this question, ensure your explanation includes these two points:
1. The value is an **arbitrary assignment by convention** to serve as a universal reference.
2. It allows all other half-cell potentials to be **measured and compared relatively**.

parts.content: Describe two standard conditions required for the operation of the hydrogen half-cell.
parts.markscheme:
- Hydrogen gas at $100\text{ kPa}$ pressure.
<marks>1 mark</marks>
- $1.00\text{ mol dm}^{-3}$ $\text{H}^+$ concentration, typically from $\text{HCl}$.
<marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Identify the half-cell components
steps.content:
To define the standard conditions, we first look at the equilibrium reaction occurring in a standard hydrogen electrode (SHE). It involves hydrogen gas and hydrogen ions in solution:

$$2\text{H}^{+}(\text{aq}) + 2e^{-} \rightleftharpoons \text{H}_{2}(\text{g})$$

Standard electrode potentials are measured against this reference cell under specific "standard state" parameters for the gas and the aqueous ions.

steps.title: State the standard pressure
steps.content:
The first condition relates to the gaseous component, **hydrogen gas**. In the IB curriculum, the standard pressure for gases is defined as $100\text{ kPa}$.

- **Condition 1:** Hydrogen gas at a pressure of $100\text{ kPa}$.

steps.title: State the standard concentration
steps.content:
The second condition relates to the aqueous component, the **hydrogen ions** ($\text{H}^{+}$). For a half-cell to be "standard," the concentration of the ions in the solution must be exactly $1.00\text{ mol dm}^{-3}$. This is typically achieved using a strong acid like $\text{HCl}$.

- **Condition 2:** $[\text{H}^{+}(\text{aq})] = 1.00\text{ mol dm}^{-3}$.

options.markscheme: In peroxides ($\mathrm{O_2^{2-}}$), oxygen is not $-2$; $-2$ applies to oxide ions ($\mathrm{O^{2-}}$).
In sodium peroxide, oxygen is present as the peroxide ion $\mathrm{O_2^{2-}}$. The total oxidation state for the two oxygens is $-2$, so each oxygen is $-1$.
Oxygen is only positive in compounds with fluorine (e.g. $\mathrm{OF_2}$) or in $\mathrm{O_2F_2}$, not in peroxides.
An oxidation state of $+2$ for oxygen does not occur in sodium peroxide; oxygen is negative in peroxides.

questions.specification:
Ruthenium, $Ru$, is a rare transition metal belonging to the platinum group of the periodic table.

Historically, it was one of the last elements of its group to be isolated, and its existence was predicted due to gaps in early periodic tables.

One common chloride of ruthenium has the empirical formula $RuCl_3$.

Ruthenium also forms oxyanions such as the perruthenate(VII) ion, $RuO_4^-$.

parts.content: Calculate the percentage, by mass, of ruthenium in $RuCl_3$.
parts.markscheme:
Calculate molar mass of $RuCl_3$:
$M(RuCl_3) = 101.07 + (3 \times 35.45) = 207.42\text{ g mol}^{-1}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

Calculate percentage by mass of ruthenium:
$\text{Percentage of Ru} = \frac{101.07}{207.42} \times 100 = 48.73\%$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content:
A researcher intends to study the catalytic activity of ruthenium when deposited as a thin film on a titanium electrode.

$$ \mathrm{Cr_2O_7^{2-}(aq) + 3Sn^{2+}(aq) + 14H^+(aq) \rightarrow 2Cr^{3+}(aq) + 3Sn^{4+}(aq) + 7H_2O(l)} $$

| **A** | $\mathrm{Cr_2O_7^{2-}(aq)}$ | $\mathrm{Sn^{2+}(aq)}$ |
| **B** | $\mathrm{Sn^{2+}(aq)}$ | $\mathrm{H^+(aq)}$ |
| **C** | $\mathrm{Sn^{2+}(aq)}$ | $\mathrm{Cr_2O_7^{2-}(aq)}$ |

options.markscheme: Correct. Cr in $\mathrm{Cr_2O_7^{2-}}$ goes from +6 to +3 (reduction), while $\mathrm{Sn^{2+}}$ goes from +2 to +4 (oxidation), so $\mathrm{Sn^{2+}}$ is the reducing agent.
Incorrect. $\mathrm{Sn^{2+}}$ is oxidized (to $\mathrm{Sn^{4+}}$), not reduced, and $\mathrm{H^+}$ is not the reducing agent.
Incorrect. $\mathrm{Cr_2O_7^{2-}}$ is reduced (to $\mathrm{Cr^{3+}}$) so it is the oxidizing agent, not the reducing agent.
Incorrect. $\mathrm{H^+}$ is neither reduced nor the reducing agent in this reaction; it provides acidic conditions for balancing.

options.markscheme: The oxidation state of phosphorus remains $$+5$$ in both species; this is a conversion between the oxide and the phosphate ion that does not involve a change in oxidation state.
The oxidation state of chlorine decreases from $$+5$$ in $$\text{ClO}_3^-$$ to $$0$$ in $$\text{Cl}_2$$, which represents a reduction.
The oxidation state of carbon remains $$+4$$ in both species; this is an acid-base reaction (conversion of the acidic oxide to its conjugate base), not a redox process.
The oxidation state of vanadium increases from $$+4$$ in $$\text{VO}^{2+}$$ to $$+5$$ in $$\text{VO}_2^+$$, which represents an oxidation.

steps.title: Understand the redox goal
steps.content:
To identify the oxidizing and reducing agents, we need to track how the oxidation states of the elements change during the reaction. 

In any redox reaction:
- The **oxidizing agent** is the substance that is **reduced** (its oxidation state decreases).
- The **reducing agent** is the substance that is **oxidized** (its oxidation state increases).

This follows the concepts found in **Topic S3.1.6: Oxidation states**.

steps.title: Assign oxidation states: Reactants
steps.content:
Let's assign oxidation states to each element in the reactants:

1.  **$\text{Zn(s)}$**: As a free element, its oxidation state is $0$.
2.  **$\text{CuSO}_4\text{(aq)}$**: This compound consists of $\text{Cu}^{2+}$ and $\text{SO}_4^{2-}$ ions. 
    - In $\text{SO}_4^{2-}$, Oxygen is $-2$ and Sulfur is $+6$.
    - To balance the $-2$ charge of the sulfate ion, **Copper ($\text{Cu}$)** must have an oxidation state of **$+2$**.

steps.title: Assign oxidation states: Products
steps.content:
Now, let's look at the products:

1.  **$\text{ZnSO}_4\text{(aq)}$**: This contains the $\text{Zn}^{2+}$ and $\text{SO}_4^{2-}$ ions. 
    - The sulfate ion remains unchanged (S is $+6$, O is $-2$).
    - To balance the $-2$ charge of the sulfate, **Zinc ($\text{Zn}$)** now has an oxidation state of **$+2$**.
2.  **$\text{Cu(s)}$**: As a free element, its oxidation state is $0$.

steps.title: Identify the agents
steps.content:
Now we compare the changes:

- **Zinc ($\text{Zn}$)**: Goes from $0 \rightarrow +2$. Since its oxidation state increased, it was oxidized. Therefore, **$\text{Zn}$ is the reducing agent**.
- **Copper ($\text{Cu}$)**: Goes from $+2 \rightarrow 0$. Since its oxidation state decreased, it was reduced. Therefore, **$\text{CuSO}_4$ (specifically the $\text{Cu}^{2+}$ ion) is the oxidizing agent**.

Note that Sulfur and Oxygen did not change their oxidation states; they are spectator atoms within the polyatomic ion.

steps.title: Write the net ionic equation
steps.content:
To understand the electron transfer, we first identify which species are changing. In aqueous solution, the compounds dissociate into ions. The sulfate ion ($\text{SO}_4^{2-}$) is a spectator ion as it remains unchanged. The net ionic equation is:

$$\text{Zn(s)} + \text{Cu}^{2+}\text{(aq)} \rightarrow \text{Zn}^{2+}\text{(aq)} + \text{Cu(s)}$$

steps.title: Separate into half-equations
steps.content:
Using the net ionic equation, we can write the two half-equations to see the movement of electrons:

**Oxidation (loss of electrons):**
$$\text{Zn(s)} \rightarrow \text{Zn}^{2+}\text{(aq)} + 2\text{e}^-$$

**Reduction (gain of electrons):**
$$\text{Cu}^{2+}\text{(aq)} + 2\text{e}^- \rightarrow \text{Cu(s)}$$

steps.title: Identify species donating electrons
steps.content:
A **reducing agent** is the species that loses (donates) electrons, thereby reducing another species. From the oxidation half-equation, we see that $\text{Zn}$ loses 2 electrons.

Therefore, $\text{Zn}$ is the **reducing agent**.

steps.title: Identify species accepting electrons
steps.content:
An **oxidizing agent** is the species that gains (accepts) electrons, thereby oxidizing another species. From the reduction half-equation, we see that the copper(II) ions gain 2 electrons.

Since these ions come from the copper(II) sulfate, $\text{CuSO}_4$ (or $\text{Cu}^{2+}$) is the **oxidizing agent**.

steps.title: Summary of findings
steps.content:
In summary:
- **Oxidizing agent:** $\text{CuSO}_4$ (or $\text{Cu}^{2+}$)
- **Reducing agent:** $\text{Zn}$

This aligns with the markscheme, where 1 mark is awarded for identifying each agent correctly.

steps.title: Compare metal reactivity
steps.content: To solve this using the reactivity series, we first compare the positions of Zinc and Copper. In the activity series of metals, Zinc is more reactive than Copper. This means Zinc has a greater tendency to lose electrons and form positive ions compared to Copper.

steps.title: Identify the reducing agent
steps.content:
Because Zinc is the more reactive metal, it will displace Copper from the solution. In this reaction, solid Zinc ($\text{Zn}$) loses two electrons to become $\text{Zn}^{2+}$ ions:

$$\text{Zn(s)} \rightarrow \text{Zn}^{2+}\text{(aq)} + 2e^-$$

Since Zinc is the species being **oxidized** (loses electrons), it is the **reducing agent**.

steps.title: Identify the oxidizing agent
steps.content:
In the reactant $\text{CuSO}_4$, Copper exists as $\text{Cu}^{2+}$ ions. These ions gain the electrons released by the Zinc to become solid Copper:

$$\text{Cu}^{2+}\text{(aq)} + 2e^- \rightarrow \text{Cu(s)}$$

Since the $\text{Cu}^{2+}$ ions (found in $\text{CuSO}_4$) are being **reduced** (gain electrons), $\text{CuSO}_4$ is the **oxidizing agent**.

steps.title: Final Conclusion
steps.content:
Based on the transfer of electrons driven by the difference in reactivity:

*   **Reducing agent:** $\text{Zn}$
*   **Oxidizing agent:** $\text{CuSO}_4$ (or $\text{Cu}^{2+}$)

This matches the markscheme requirements for both points.

parts.content: Write the half-reactions for the oxidation and reduction processes occurring in this reaction.
parts.markscheme:
Oxidation half-reaction:
$$\text{Zn(s)} \rightarrow \text{Zn}^{2+}\text{(aq)} + 2\text{e}^-$$ <marks>2</marks>

Reduction half-reaction:
$$\text{Cu}^{2+}\text{(aq)} + 2\text{e}^- \rightarrow \text{Cu(s)}$$ <marks>2</marks>

<note>Award marks for correct half-reactions even if agents were incorrectly identified in Part 1.</note>

steps.title: Identify ions and spectators
steps.content:
To write half-reactions, we first look at the ionic species involved in the reaction. In the equation:

$$\text{Zn(s)} + \text{CuSO}_4\text{(aq)} \rightarrow \text{ZnSO}_4\text{(aq)} + \text{Cu(s)}$$

The aqueous compounds dissociate into ions. Specifically, the sulfate ion ($\text{SO}_4^{2-}$) appears on both sides unchanged, making it a spectator ion. We focus on the net ionic species:

$$\text{Zn(s)} + \text{Cu}^{2+}\text{(aq)} \rightarrow \text{Zn}^{2+}\text{(aq)} + \text{Cu(s)}$$

steps.title: Assign oxidation states
steps.content:
Using the rules for oxidation states (S3.1.6), we assign values to each element:

*   **Zn(s)**: $0$ (elements in their standard state have an oxidation state of $0$)
*   **Cu$^{2+}$(aq)**: $+2$ (the oxidation state of a monoatomic ion equals its charge)
*   **Zn$^{2+}$(aq)**: $+2$
*   **Cu(s)**: $0$

Notice that Zinc's oxidation state increases, while Copper's decreases.

steps.title: Write oxidation half-reaction
steps.content:
Oxidation is defined as an increase in oxidation state, which occurs through the loss of electrons (**OIL**: Oxidation Is Loss). 

Zinc goes from $0$ to $+2$, meaning it loses $2$ electrons:

$$\text{Zn(s)} \rightarrow \text{Zn}^{2+}\text{(aq)} + 2\text{e}^-$$

This is the oxidation half-reaction. In the markscheme, providing this complete equation earns **2 marks**.

steps.title: Write reduction half-reaction
steps.content:
Reduction is defined as a decrease in oxidation state, which occurs through the gain of electrons (**RIG**: Reduction Is Gain). 

Copper goes from $+2$ to $0$, meaning it gains $2$ electrons:

$$\text{Cu}^{2+}\text{(aq)} + 2\text{e}^- \rightarrow \text{Cu(s)}$$

This is the reduction half-reaction. Providing this complete equation earns the remaining **2 marks**.

steps.title: Analyze the chemical equation
steps.content:
To find the half-reactions, we first look at the state symbols in the provided molecular equation. We can see that $\text{CuSO}_4$ and $\text{ZnSO}_4$ are aqueous (aq), meaning they exist as dissociated ions in solution. This is the first step in the **Net Ionic Equation Decomposition** approach.

$$\text{Zn(s)} + \text{CuSO}_4\text{(aq)} \rightarrow \text{ZnSO}_4\text{(aq)} + \text{Cu(s)}$$

steps.title: Dissociate aqueous compounds
steps.content:
We rewrite the equation by splitting the aqueous compounds into their respective ions. This helps us see which species are actually changing and which are just "watching" the reaction.

$$\text{Zn(s)} + \text{Cu}^{2+}\text{(aq)} + \text{SO}_4^{2-}\text{(aq)} \rightarrow \text{Zn}^{2+}\text{(aq)} + \text{SO}_4^{2-}\text{(aq)} + \text{Cu(s)}$$

steps.title: Identify and remove spectators
steps.content:
Notice that the sulfate ion, $\text{SO}_4^{2-}$, appears on both the reactant and product sides in the exact same form. These are **spectator ions**. By removing them, we obtain the net ionic equation:

$$\text{Zn(s)} + \text{Cu}^{2+}\text{(aq)} \rightarrow \text{Zn}^{2+}\text{(aq)} + \text{Cu(s)}$$

This simplified equation shows the actual electron transfer occurring between the metal species.

steps.title: Decompose into metal processes
steps.content:
Now, we decompose the net ionic equation into two separate processes based on the individual metals:

1.  **Zinc process**: $\text{Zn(s)} \rightarrow \text{Zn}^{2+}\text{(aq)}$
2.  **Copper process**: $\text{Cu}^{2+}\text{(aq)} \rightarrow \text{Cu(s)}$

To turn these into proper half-reactions, we must balance the electric charge by adding electrons ($e^-$).

steps.title: Balance the Zinc half-reaction
steps.content:
In the Zinc process, the charge goes from $0$ on the left to $+2$ on the right. To balance this, we must add two electrons to the product side. Since loss of electrons is **oxidation** (OIL RIG), this is the oxidation half-reaction.

**Oxidation:**
$$\text{Zn(s)} \rightarrow \text{Zn}^{2+}\text{(aq)} + 2\text{e}^-$$

gdc.instructions: Evaluate the net charge on the product side.

ti84.instructions: Calculate the sum of charges on the right side.

desmos.instructions: Sum the oxidation state and electron charges.

calculator.description: Verify charge balance for Zinc: 0 = 2 + (-2)

steps.title: Balance the Copper half-reaction
steps.content:
In the Copper process, the charge goes from $+2$ on the left to $0$ on the right. To balance this, we add two electrons to the reactant side. Since gain of electrons is **reduction**, this is the reduction half-reaction.

**Reduction:**
$$\text{Cu}^{2+}\text{(aq)} + 2\text{e}^- \rightarrow \text{Cu(s)}$$

gdc.instructions: Evaluate the net charge on the reactant side.

ti84.instructions: Calculate the sum of charges on the left side.

desmos.instructions: Sum the ion charge and electron charges.

calculator.description: Verify charge balance for Copper: 2 + (-2) = 0

steps.title: Final Answer
steps.content:
The completed half-reactions are:

**Oxidation half-reaction:**
$$\text{Zn(s)} \rightarrow \text{Zn}^{2+}\text{(aq)} + 2\text{e}^-$$

**Reduction half-reaction:**
$$\text{Cu}^{2+}\text{(aq)} + 2\text{e}^- \rightarrow \text{Cu(s)}$$

In an IB exam, ensure you include the state symbols if they are provided in the stem of the question, as they are often required for full marks.

steps.title: Identify the reacting species
steps.content:
First, we look at the provided chemical equation to identify the species involved in the transfer of electrons. 

$$\text{Zn(s)} + \text{CuSO}_4\text{(aq)} \rightarrow \text{ZnSO}_4\text{(aq)} + \text{Cu(s)}$$

In aqueous solution, ionic compounds dissociate. We focus on the metal species:
- **Zinc** starts as a solid metal $\text{Zn(s)}$ and becomes $\text{Zn}^{2+}(\text{aq})$ in $\text{ZnSO}_4$.
- **Copper** starts as $\text{Cu}^{2+}(\text{aq})$ in $\text{CuSO}_4$ and becomes solid metal $\text{Cu(s)}$.

This involves **Topic R3.2: Electron transfer reactions**, which defines redox processes by the movement of electrons.

steps.title: Consult the Data Booklet
steps.content:
The IB Data Booklet contains a table of **Standard Electrode Potentials**. All equations in this table are written as **reductions** (gaining electrons). We find the two relevant entries:

1.  $$\text{Zn}^{2+}(\text{aq}) + 2\text{e}^- \rightleftharpoons \text{Zn}(\text{s})$$
2.  $$\text{Cu}^{2+}(\text{aq}) + 2\text{e}^- \rightleftharpoons \text{Cu}(\text{s})$$

These provide the basic 'building blocks' for our half-reactions.

steps.title: Determine oxidation and reduction
steps.content:
By comparing the reactants in the question to the Data Booklet equations, we determine the direction of electron flow:

- **Reduction** is the gain of electrons. The reaction shows $\text{Cu}^{2+}$ becoming $\text{Cu}$, which matches the reduction format in the booklet.
- **Oxidation** is the loss of electrons. The reaction shows $\text{Zn}$ becoming $\text{Zn}^{2+}$. This is the **reverse** of the equation found in the booklet.

steps.title: Write the final half-reactions
steps.content:
We now write the final equations. For the oxidation process, we must reverse the Zinc equation from the Data Booklet so that the electrons appear on the product side.

**Oxidation half-reaction (2 marks):**
$$\text{Zn(s)} \rightarrow \text{Zn}^{2+}(\text{aq}) + 2\text{e}^-$$

**Reduction half-reaction (2 marks):**
$$\text{Cu}^{2+}(\text{aq}) + 2\text{e}^- \rightarrow \text{Cu(s)}$$

Ensure that both charge and atoms are balanced in each equation. For example, in the oxidation half-reaction, the total charge on both sides is zero ($0 = +2 + (-2)$).

questions.specification:
A student investigates the reactivity of three metals (zinc, copper, and silver) by placing each in separate solutions containing different metal ions. The goal is to determine their relative reactivity using displacement reactions.

Consider the following extract from the reactivity series, from more reactive to less reactive:

$\text{Ca} > \text{Zn} > \text{Co} > \text{Cu} > \text{Ag}$

labeledSubSubTopics.title: R3.2.1 Oxidation and reduction definitions

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Reactivity 3. What are the mechanisms of chemical change? > R3.2 Electron transfer reactions > R3.2.3 Reactivity of elements based on periodic trends
labeledSubSubTopics.title: R3.2.3 Reactivity of elements based on periodic trends
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question uses a reactivity series to predict which metals will displace Ag+ and whether Cu will displace Zn2+, linking reactivity to tendency to lose electrons (periodic/element reactivity ideas).

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Reactivity 3. What are the mechanisms of chemical change? > R3.2 Electron transfer reactions > R3.2.2 Redox half-equations

parts.content: Predict which of these metals will displace $\text{Ag}^+$ from aqueous solution.
parts.markscheme: Zinc and copper will displace $\text{Ag}^+$. <marks>1</marks>

steps.title: Understand the displacement rule
steps.content:
In a displacement reaction, a more reactive metal will "push out" (displace) a less reactive metal from its solution. To figure out which metals can displace $\text{Ag}^{+}$, we look at the **reactivity series**. 

Metals listed to the left (or higher up) are more reactive than those to the right. This concept is a key part of **Topic R3.2: Electron transfer reactions** in your syllabus.

steps.title: Locate Silver in the series
steps.content:
Let's look at the specific extract provided in the question:

$$\text{Ca} > \text{Zn} > \text{Co} > \text{Cu} > \text{Ag}$$

Notice that $\text{Ag}$ (silver) is at the very end of this list. This means any metal positioned to the left of silver in this sequence is more reactive than silver and has the power to displace it.

steps.title: Compare the investigated metals
steps.content:
The student is specifically investigating three metals: **zinc**, **copper**, and **silver**. 

Using the sequence, we can see where they sit relative to $\text{Ag}$:
- $\text{Zn}$ (zinc) is to the left of $\text{Ag}$.
- $\text{Cu}$ (copper) is to the left of $\text{Ag}$.
- $\text{Ag}$ (silver) is the reference metal itself.

Since both zinc and copper are higher (more reactive) than silver, they will both react with the solution to displace $\text{Ag}^{+}$ ions.

steps.title: Final prediction
steps.content:
Based on our comparison, the metals that will displace $\text{Ag}^{+}$ are zinc and copper.

Silver metal will not displace silver ions because it has the same reactivity as the ions in solution.

steps.title: Understand displacement and potentials
steps.content: In a displacement reaction, a more reactive metal reduces the ions of a less reactive metal. Using the **Standard Electrode Potentials** approach, a metal can displace $\text{Ag}^+$ if it has a **lower (more negative)** standard reduction potential ($E^{\ominus}$) than silver. This means the metal is a stronger reducing agent.

steps.title: Identify electrode potentials
steps.content:
We need to look up the standard reduction potentials ($E^{\ominus}$) for the three metals in the IB Data Booklet. The relevant half-equations are:

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Ag}^+(aq) + e^- &\rightleftharpoons \text{Ag}(s) && E^{\ominus} = +0.80\text{ V} \\ \text{Cu}^{2+}(aq) + 2e^- &\rightleftharpoons \text{Cu}(s) && E^{\ominus} = +0.34\text{ V} \\ \text{Zn}^{2+}(aq) + 2e^- &\rightleftharpoons \text{Zn}(s) && E^{\ominus} = -0.76\text{ V} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Compare $E^{\ominus}$ values
steps.content:
For a reaction to be spontaneous, the metal being added must have a lower $E^{\ominus}$ than the ion in solution ($+0.80\text{ V}$ for $\text{Ag}^+$):

*   **Zinc:** $-0.76\text{ V} < +0.80\text{ V}$ (Spontaneous)
*   **Copper:** $+0.34\text{ V} < +0.80\text{ V}$ (Spontaneous)

Both zinc and copper are stronger reducing agents than silver and will therefore displace it from solution.

steps.title: Final Conclusion
steps.content: Based on the standard electrode potentials, both **zinc and copper** will displace $\text{Ag}^+$. This matches the reactivity series extract provided, which shows both metals are more reactive than silver.

highlights.text: Zinc and copper will displace Ag^+

parts.content: Explain your answer in terms of electron transfer and relative reactivity.
parts.markscheme:
- Zinc and copper are more reactive and act as reducing agents, donating electrons <marks>1</marks>
- $\text{Ag}^+$ is reduced to $\text{Ag}(\text{s})$, while zinc or copper are oxidized <marks>1</marks>

steps.title: Identify relative reactivity
steps.content:
To explain the reaction, we first look at the provided reactivity series. Metals higher in the series (further to the left) are more reactive than those lower down. 

$$\text{Ca} > \text{Zn} > \text{Co} > \text{Cu} > \text{Ag}$$

Since zinc ($\text{Zn}$) and copper ($\text{Cu}$) are placed to the left of silver ($\text{Ag}$), they are both more reactive than silver.

steps.title: Explain reducing agents
steps.content:
In chemistry, a more reactive metal has a greater tendency to lose electrons. Therefore, zinc and copper act as **reducing agents** because they donate electrons to the less reactive metal ions in the solution.

This process of losing electrons is called **oxidation**. For example, the oxidation of zinc can be represented as:

$$\text{Zn}(\text{s}) \rightarrow \text{Zn}^{2+}(\text{aq}) + 2\text{e}^-$$

steps.title: Explain electron transfer
steps.content:
The electrons donated by the more reactive metal are accepted by the silver ions ($\text{Ag}^+$) in the solution. This gain of electrons is called **reduction**. 

The silver ions are reduced to solid silver metal, which often appears as a precipitate or coating on the more reactive metal:

$$\text{Ag}^+(\text{aq}) + \text{e}^- \rightarrow \text{Ag}(\text{s})$$

steps.title: Final Summary
steps.content:
To earn full marks, your explanation must connect the relative reactivity to the electron flow:

1.  **Reactivity & Agents**: Zinc and copper are more reactive than silver, meaning they are stronger reducing agents that donate electrons.
2.  **Redox process**: The silver ions ($\text{Ag}^+$) are reduced to silver metal ($\text{Ag}$), while the more reactive metal (zinc or copper) is oxidized into ions.

steps.title: Identify relative reactivity
steps.content:
From the provided reactivity series, we can see that both zinc ($\text{Zn}$) and copper ($\text{Cu}$) are positioned to the left of silver ($\text{Ag}$):

$$\text{Ca} > \text{Zn} > \text{Co} > \text{Cu} > \text{Ag}$$

This means zinc and copper are more reactive than silver. In a displacement reaction, the more reactive metal will lose electrons more readily than the less reactive one. This concept is a core part of **Topic R3.2: Electron transfer reactions**.

steps.title: Analyze oxidation half-equations
steps.content:
Because zinc and copper are more reactive, they act as **reducing agents**. This means they donate electrons to the silver ions. When a substance loses electrons, it undergoes **oxidation**.

The symbolic half-equations for these oxidation processes are:

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Zn(s)} &\rightarrow \text{Zn}^{2+}\text{(aq)} + 2e^- \\ \text{Cu(s)} &\rightarrow \text{Cu}^{2+}\text{(aq)} + 2e^- \end{aligned}$$

Here, the solid metal atoms lose electrons to become aqueous ions.

steps.title: Analyze reduction half-equation
steps.content:
The silver ions ($\text{Ag}^+$) in the solution accept the electrons donated by the more reactive metal. When a substance gains electrons, it undergoes **reduction**.

The symbolic half-equation for the reduction of silver is:

$$\text{Ag}^+\text{(aq)} + e^- \rightarrow \text{Ag(s)}$$

This results in the formation of solid silver metal on the surface of the zinc or copper.

steps.title: Synthesize the final explanation
steps.content:
To earn full marks, you must link the reactivity to the electron transfer process:

1.  **Reactivity and Agents**: Zinc and copper are more reactive than silver, so they act as reducing agents by donating electrons.
2.  **Redox process**: The $\text{Ag}^+$ ions are reduced to silver metal ($\text{Ag(s)}$), while the zinc or copper atoms are oxidized into their respective ions.

parts.content: Write a balanced ionic equation for one of the successful displacement reactions.
parts.markscheme:
Example: $\text{Cu}(\text{s}) + 2\text{Ag}^+(\text{aq}) \rightarrow \text{Cu}^{2+}(\text{aq}) + 2\text{Ag}(\text{s})$

- Correct species <marks>1</marks>
- Equation balanced in mass and charge <marks>1</marks>

steps.title: Select the reaction
steps.content: To write a successful displacement reaction, we must choose a more reactive metal to displace a less reactive metal ion. Looking at the provided reactivity series, we can see that zinc is more reactive than copper.

steps.title: Identify ions and charges
steps.content:
In this specific approach, we use zinc ($\text{Zn}$) to displace copper(II) ions ($\text{Cu}^{2+}$). 

*   Zinc atoms lose electrons to form $\text{Zn}^{2+}$ ions.
*   Copper(II) ions gain electrons to form solid copper ($\text{Cu}$).

Since both metals form $2+$ ions, they will react in a simple $1:1$ ratio to maintain charge balance.

steps.title: Write the balanced equation
steps.content:
We combine the species into a single ionic equation. We must include state symbols: (s) for solids and (aq) for aqueous ions.

$$\text{Zn}(\text{s}) + \text{Cu}^{2+}(\text{aq}) \rightarrow \text{Zn}^{2+}(\text{aq}) + \text{Cu}(\text{s})$$

This equation earns full marks because it contains the **correct species** and is **balanced in mass and charge**.

gdc.instructions: Enter the sum of the charges for the reactants and then for the products to ensure they are equal.

ti84.instructions: Sum the charges on the left side (0 for Zn, +2 for Cu) and the right side (+2 for Zn, 0 for Cu).

desmos.instructions:
Verify the sum of charges: 
LHS: 0 + 2
RHS: 2 + 0

calculator.description: You can verify the charge balance by summing the oxidation states on each side of the equation. Both sides should equal the same total value.

highlights.text: Equation balanced in mass and charge

steps.title: Identify a successful displacement
steps.content:
A displacement reaction occurs when a more reactive metal is placed in a solution containing ions of a less reactive metal. According to the reactivity series provided, copper ($\text{Cu}$) is more reactive than silver ($\text{Ag}$).

$$\text{Ca} > \text{Zn} > \text{Co} > \text{Cu} > \text{Ag}$$

This means solid copper will successfully displace silver ions ($\text{Ag}^{+}$) from a solution.

steps.title: Determine ion charges
steps.content:
To write the ionic equation, we need the charges of the ions involved. 
- Copper forms the divalent cation: $\text{Cu}^{2+}$
- Silver forms the monovalent cation: $\text{Ag}^{+}$

You can find these concepts in **Topic R3.2: Electron transfer reactions**, which explains how atoms lose or gain electrons to become ions.

steps.title: Balance the charge
steps.content:
In a balanced ionic equation, the total charge on the reactant side must equal the total charge on the product side. 

One copper atom loses **two** electrons to become $\text{Cu}^{2+}$. However, one silver ion only needs **one** electron to become a solid silver atom. To balance the exchange of electrons, we must use two silver ions for every one copper atom.

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Total reactant charge: } & 0 + 2(+1) = +2 \\ \text{Total product charge: } & +2 + 2(0) = +2 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Write the final equation
steps.content:
Combining the species with their correct 1:2 stoichiometry and including state symbols gives us the final equation. This earns both marks: one for correct species and one for correct balancing of mass and charge.

$$\text{Cu}(\text{s}) + 2\text{Ag}^+(\text{aq}) \rightarrow \text{Cu}^{2+}(\text{aq}) + 2\text{Ag}(\text{s})$$

highlights.text: Equation balanced in mass and charge

steps.title: Identify a valid reaction
steps.content:
A displacement reaction occurs when a more reactive metal is placed into a solution containing ions of a less reactive metal. Looking at the provided reactivity series:

$$\text{Ca} > \text{Zn} > \text{Co} > \text{Cu} > \text{Ag}$$

We can choose any pair where the solid metal is higher in the series than the metal ion. Let's use **copper (Cu)** and **silver ions (Ag⁺)** for this example, as copper is more reactive than silver.

steps.title: Write the oxidation half-equation
steps.content:
Oxidation is the loss of electrons. In this reaction, the more reactive solid metal atoms (Cu) lose electrons to become aqueous ions ($$\text{Cu}^{2+}$$).

$$\text{Cu}(\text{s}) \rightarrow \text{Cu}^{2+}(\text{aq}) + 2\text{e}^-$$

steps.title: Write the reduction half-equation
steps.content:
Reduction is the gain of electrons. The less reactive metal ions ($$\text{Ag}^+$$) in the solution gain electrons to become solid metal atoms (Ag).

$$\text{Ag}^+(\text{aq}) + \text{e}^- \rightarrow \text{Ag}(\text{s})$$

steps.title: Balance the electron transfer
steps.content:
To combine the half-equations, the number of electrons lost in oxidation must equal the number of electrons gained in reduction. 

Since copper loses 2 electrons but silver only gains 1, we must multiply the silver reduction half-equation by 2:

$$2\text{Ag}^+(\text{aq}) + 2\text{e}^- \rightarrow 2\text{Ag}(\text{s})$$

steps.title: Combine and simplify
steps.content:
Now, we add the two half-equations together and cancel out the electrons on both sides:

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Cu}(\text{s}) &\rightarrow \text{Cu}^{2+}(\text{aq}) + 2\text{e}^- \\ 2\text{Ag}^+(\text{aq}) + 2\text{e}^- &\rightarrow 2\text{Ag}(\text{s}) \\ \hline \text{Cu}(\text{s}) + 2\text{Ag}^+(\text{aq}) &\rightarrow \text{Cu}^{2+}(\text{aq}) + 2\text{Ag}(\text{s}) \end{aligned}$$

This equation is balanced by both mass (1 Cu, 2 Ag on each side) and charge (total charge of +2 on both sides).

parts.content: State and explain whether a displacement reaction would occur if copper metal were added to a $\text{Zn}^{2+}$ solution.
parts.markscheme:
- No reaction would occur <marks>1</marks>
- Copper is less reactive than zinc, so cannot reduce $\text{Zn}^{2+}$ <marks>1</marks>

steps.title: Identify the reactants
steps.content: In this scenario, we are adding solid copper metal ($Cu$) to a solution containing zinc ions ($Zn^{2+}$). To determine if a displacement reaction happens, we need to compare the reactivity of these two metals. This concept is part of **Topic R3.2: Electron transfer reactions**.

highlights.text: copper metal were added to a Zn^{2+} solution

steps.title: Analyze the reactivity series
steps.content:
Let's look at the reactivity series provided in the question. It lists metals from most reactive to least reactive:

$$\text{Ca} > \text{Zn} > \text{Co} > \text{Cu} > \text{Ag}$$

Notice the positions of Zinc ($Zn$) and Copper ($Cu$). Zinc is listed earlier in the sequence, which means it is more reactive than copper.

steps.title: Apply the displacement rule
steps.content:
For a displacement reaction to occur, the solid metal added must be **more reactive** than the metal already in the solution. This is because a more reactive metal has a greater tendency to lose electrons (be oxidized) and reduce the ions of a less reactive metal.

Since copper is **less reactive** than zinc, it does not have enough "driving force" to push its electrons onto the zinc ions.

steps.title: State the final conclusion
steps.content:
Because copper cannot displace zinc from the solution, **no reaction would occur**. 

In an exam, you earn one mark for stating there is no reaction and a second mark for explaining that copper is less reactive than zinc.

highlights.text: Copper is less reactive than zinc, so cannot reduce Zn^{2+}

steps.title: Analyze the reactivity series
steps.content:
To determine if a reaction occurs, we first look at the relative positions of zinc and copper in the provided reactivity series. Metals higher in the series are more reactive than those lower down.

$$\text{Ca} > \text{Zn} > \text{Co} > \text{Cu} > \text{Ag}$$

From the list, we can see that zinc ($\text{Zn}$) is placed higher than copper ($\text{Cu}$), meaning zinc is the more reactive metal of the two.

steps.title: Define redox principles
steps.content:
In chemistry, 'reactivity' for metals describes their ability to lose electrons and undergo oxidation. A more reactive metal is a **stronger reducing agent**.

Since zinc is more reactive than copper, it is a stronger reducing agent. This means zinc atoms have a much greater tendency to lose electrons to form ions than copper atoms do.

steps.title: Evaluate electron transfer
steps.content:
A displacement reaction is essentially a competition for electrons. For a reaction to occur in this scenario, the copper metal ($Cu$) would have to donate electrons to the zinc ions ($Zn^{2+}$):

$$Cu(s) + Zn^{2+}(aq) \rightarrow Cu^{2+}(aq) + Zn(s)$$

However, because copper is a **weaker reducing agent** than zinc, it does not have enough 'power' to force its electrons onto the $Zn^{2+}$ ions. Copper cannot reduce $Zn^{2+}$ back to its elemental metallic form.

steps.title: State the final conclusion
steps.content:
Since copper is less reactive than zinc, it cannot displace zinc from its solution. Therefore, **no reaction occurs**.

In an exam, you earn one mark for stating there is no reaction and a second mark for explaining that copper is less reactive (a weaker reducing agent) than zinc.

parts.content: Identify the components labeled 1, 2, 3, and 4 in a $\mathrm{Zn} \ | \ \mathrm{Cu}$ electrochemical cell.
parts.markscheme:
- Identify labels 1 and 2: 1 is $\mathrm{ZnSO}_4$ solution (or $\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}\mathrm{(aq)}$) and 2 is the zinc electrode $\mathrm{(Zn)}$ <marks>A1</marks>
- Identify labels 3 and 4: 3 is the copper electrode $\mathrm{(Cu)}$ and 4 is $\mathrm{CuSO}_4$ solution (or $\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}\mathrm{(aq)}$) <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Compare reactivity and assign roles
steps.content:
To identify the components of a $\mathrm{Zn} \ | \ \mathrm{Cu}$ electrochemical cell, we first look at the reactivity of the two metals. 

Zinc $(\mathrm{Zn})$ is more reactive than copper $(\mathrm{Cu})$. In a voltaic cell, the more reactive metal acts as the **anode** (where oxidation occurs), and the less reactive metal acts as the **cathode** (where reduction occurs). By convention in standard cell diagrams, the anode is placed on the left and the cathode on the right.

This concept is covered in **Topic R3.2: Electron transfer reactions**, which explains how differences in reactivity drive the flow of electrons.

steps.title: Identify labels 1 and 2
steps.content:
The left half-cell contains the more reactive metal, zinc. 

*   **Label 2** points to the solid metal strip, which is the **zinc electrode** $(\mathrm{Zn})$.
*   **Label 1** points to the liquid in the beaker. For the cell to function, the electrode must be dipped in a solution containing its own ions. Therefore, 1 is the **zinc sulfate solution** $(\mathrm{ZnSO}_4)$ or simply aqueous zinc ions $(\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}\mathrm{(aq)})$.

highlights.text: Identify labels 1 and 2: 1 is ZnSO4 solution (or Zn2+(aq)) and 2 is the zinc electrode (Zn)

steps.title: Identify labels 3 and 4
steps.content:
The right half-cell contains the less reactive metal, copper.

*   **Label 3** points to the solid metal strip, which is the **copper electrode** $(\mathrm{Cu})$.
*   **Label 4** points to the liquid surrounding the copper electrode. Similar to the first beaker, this must contain copper ions. Therefore, 4 is the **copper(II) sulfate solution** $(\mathrm{CuSO}_4)$ or aqueous copper ions $(\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}\mathrm{(aq)})$. 

In the exam, mentioning either the full salt name or the ion is usually enough to secure the mark.

highlights.text: Identify labels 3 and 4: 3 is the copper electrode (Cu) and 4 is CuSO4 solution (or Cu2+(aq))

steps.title: Final answer summary
steps.content:
Based on our identification of the anode and cathode components:

*   **1:** $\mathrm{ZnSO}_4$ solution / $\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}\mathrm{(aq)}$
*   **2:** Zinc electrode $(\mathrm{Zn})$
*   **3:** Copper electrode $(\mathrm{Cu})$
*   **4:** $\mathrm{CuSO}_4$ solution / $\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}\mathrm{(aq)}$

parts.content: State the direction of electron flow in the external circuit.
parts.markscheme:
- State that electrons flow from the zinc electrode (2) to the copper electrode (3) <marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

steps.title: Consult the activity series
steps.content: To determine the direction of electron flow, we first compare the reactivity of the two metals using the activity series. Zinc ($Zn$) is located higher in the activity series than Copper ($Cu$), meaning zinc is the more reactive metal and more easily oxidized.

steps.title: Identify the oxidation site
steps.content:
In a voltaic cell, the more reactive metal acts as the **anode**, where oxidation (loss of electrons) occurs. The zinc electrode (labeled 2) undergoes oxidation:

$$\text{Zn(s)} \rightarrow \text{Zn}^{2+}\text{(aq)} + 2\text{e}^{-}$$

steps.title: Determine electron flow direction
steps.content:
The electrons released at the zinc anode travel through the external circuit (the wire) to reach the copper electrode (labeled 3), which acts as the cathode. 

Therefore, the electrons flow from the **zinc electrode (2)** to the **copper electrode (3)**.

steps.title: Identify the electrodes
steps.content: To determine the direction of electron flow, we first need to identify the metals used in the electrochemical cell. Looking at the diagram, we have a zinc electrode labeled as **2** and a copper electrode labeled as **3**.

steps.title: Consult standard electrode potentials
steps.content:
Using the IB Chemistry Data Booklet (Section 24), we can find the standard electrode potentials ($E^{\ominus}$) for the two half-reactions:

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Zn}^{2+}(\text{aq}) + 2e^- &\rightleftharpoons \text{Zn}(\text{s}) & E^{\ominus} &= -0.76 \text{ V} \\ \text{Cu}^{2+}(\text{aq}) + 2e^- &\rightleftharpoons \text{Cu}(\text{s}) & E^{\ominus} &= +0.34 \text{ V} \end{aligned}$$

These values tell us how easily a species is reduced (gains electrons).

steps.title: Compare electrode potentials
steps.content:
In a voltaic cell, electrons always flow from the half-cell with the **more negative** (lower) $E^{\ominus}$ to the half-cell with the **more positive** (higher) $E^{\ominus}$.

Comparing our values:
- Zinc ($E^{\ominus} = -0.76 \text{ V}$) is more negative.
- Copper ($E^{\ominus} = +0.34 \text{ V}$) is more positive.

Since zinc has a more negative potential, it has a greater tendency to lose electrons (oxidation) compared to copper.

steps.title: State the flow direction
steps.content:
Because the zinc electrode is the site of oxidation, it releases electrons into the external circuit. These electrons then travel through the wire toward the copper electrode where reduction occurs.

Therefore, the electrons flow from the **zinc electrode (2)** to the **copper electrode (3)**.

highlights.text: zinc electrode (2) to the copper electrode (3)

parts.content: Outline the purpose of the salt bridge in the electrochemical cell.
parts.markscheme:
- State that the salt bridge allows the flow of ions to maintain electrical neutrality <marks>A1</marks>
- State that it completes the electrical circuit <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify the component
steps.content: In the diagram of the electrochemical cell (also known as a voltaic or galvanic cell), the part labeled **2** is the salt bridge. This component is essential for the cell to generate a continuous flow of electricity. To understand its role, we look at the two functions defined in **Topic R3.2: Electron transfer reactions**.

steps.title: Maintain electrical neutrality
steps.content:
As the redox reaction occurs, oxidation at the anode produces positive ions (e.g., $Zn^{2+}$), while reduction at the cathode removes positive ions from the solution. This creates a charge imbalance. 

The salt bridge contains an inert electrolyte (like $KNO_3$) that allows the **flow of ions** into the half-cells. Negative ions (anions) move toward the anode and positive ions (cations) move toward the cathode to **maintain electrical neutrality**.

steps.title: Complete the circuit
steps.content: For current to flow, there must be a continuous loop. While electrons move through the external wire (part 1), the salt bridge provides the internal pathway for charge to travel between the two beakers. By allowing ions to migrate, it **completes the electrical circuit**.

steps.title: Final Summary
steps.content:
To earn both marks on your IB exam, ensure you mention these two distinct roles:

1.  It allows the **flow of ions** to **maintain electrical neutrality** in the half-cells.
2.  It **completes the electrical circuit**.

parts.content: Write the half-equations for the reactions occurring at each electrode.
parts.markscheme:
- Write the oxidation half-equation at the zinc electrode: $\mathrm{Zn(s)} \to \mathrm{Zn}^{2+}\mathrm{(aq)} + 2\mathrm{e}^-$ <marks>A1</marks>
- Write the reduction half-equation at the copper electrode: $\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}\mathrm{(aq)} + 2\mathrm{e}^- \to \mathrm{Cu(s)}$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Locate standard electrode potentials
steps.content:
To determine which reaction occurs at each electrode, we look up the standard reduction potentials ($E^{\ominus}$) in the IB Data Booklet. For this cell, we need the values for zinc and copper:

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Zn}^{2+}\text{(aq)} + 2\text{e}^- &\rightleftharpoons \text{Zn(s)} && E^{\ominus} = -0.76\text{ V} \\ \text{Cu}^{2+}\text{(aq)} + 2\text{e}^- &\rightleftharpoons \text{Cu(s)} && E^{\ominus} = +0.34\text{ V} \end{aligned}$$

This follows the concepts found in **Topic R3.2: Electron transfer reactions**.

steps.title: Identify oxidation and reduction
steps.content:
In a voltaic cell, the species with the **more negative** $E^{\ominus}$ value is the stronger reducing agent and will undergo **oxidation** at the anode. The species with the **more positive** $E^{\ominus}$ value is the stronger oxidizing agent and will undergo **reduction** at the cathode.

*   **Zinc** ($-0.76\text{ V}$) is more negative than copper ($+0.34\text{ V}$), so Zinc metal is oxidized.
*   **Copper ions** are reduced as they have a more positive potential.

steps.title: Write zinc oxidation equation
steps.content:
Since zinc is being oxidized, we reverse the reduction equation found in the data booklet. Oxidation is the loss of electrons, so the electrons appear on the product side.

$$\text{Zn(s)} \to \text{Zn}^{2+}\text{(aq)} + 2\text{e}^-$$

steps.title: Write copper reduction equation
steps.content:
Copper ions undergo reduction at the cathode. Reduction is the gain of electrons, so we use the equation exactly as it appears in the reduction potential table.

$$\text{Cu}^{2+}\text{(aq)} + 2\text{e}^- \to \text{Cu(s)}$$

steps.title: Final Summary
steps.content:
The final half-equations are:

**At the zinc electrode (Anode):**
$$\text{Zn(s)} \to \text{Zn}^{2+}\text{(aq)} + 2\text{e}^-$$

**At the copper electrode (Cathode):**
$$\text{Cu}^{2+}\text{(aq)} + 2\text{e}^- \to \text{Cu(s)}$$

You earn one mark for each correctly balanced half-equation including the correct direction of electron flow.

steps.title: Identify the metals
steps.content: In this electrochemical cell, we have two different metal electrodes: zinc (Zn) and copper (Cu). To figure out what reactions happen, we first need to determine which metal is more reactive by looking at the **Activity Series** (found in Section 25 of your Data Booklet).

steps.title: Compare reactivity
steps.content:
Looking at the Activity Series, zinc is positioned higher than copper. This means zinc is more reactive and has a greater tendency to lose electrons (oxidize) than copper. 

In a voltaic cell:
- The **more reactive metal** (Zinc) will be the **anode** where **oxidation** occurs.
- The **less reactive metal** (Copper) will be the **cathode** where **reduction** occurs.

steps.title: Write zinc oxidation equation
steps.content:
At the zinc electrode (the anode), solid zinc atoms lose two electrons to form zinc ions. This is an oxidation reaction because electrons are lost.

$$\text{Zn(s)} \rightarrow \text{Zn}^{2+}\text{(aq)} + 2\text{e}^-$$

highlights.text: Write the oxidation half-equation at the zinc electrode: $\mathrm{Zn(s)} \to \mathrm{Zn}^{2+}\mathrm{(aq)} + 2\mathrm{e}^-$

steps.title: Write copper reduction equation
steps.content:
At the copper electrode (the cathode), the copper ions in the solution gain the electrons that traveled through the wire from the zinc side. These ions are reduced to form solid copper metal.

$$\text{Cu}^{2+}\text{(aq)} + 2\text{e}^- \rightarrow \text{Cu(s)}$$

highlights.text: Write the reduction half-equation at the copper electrode: $\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}\mathrm{(aq)} + 2\mathrm{e}^- \to \mathrm{Cu(s)}$

steps.title: Final answer summary
steps.content:
To get full marks, ensure you include the state symbols and show the correct number of electrons balanced for both equations:

**Zinc electrode (Oxidation):** 
$$\text{Zn(s)} \rightarrow \text{Zn}^{2+}\text{(aq)} + 2\text{e}^-$$

**Copper electrode (Reduction):** 
$$\text{Cu}^{2+}\text{(aq)} + 2\text{e}^- \rightarrow \text{Cu(s)}$$

questions.specification:
A student investigates the relative reactivity of four unknown metals: $W, X, Y,$ and $Z$. Small samples of each metal were placed into separate test tubes containing aqueous solutions of their nitrate salts. The observations are recorded in the table below:

| Experiment | Reactants | Observation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $X(s) + YNO_3(aq)$ | A coating of metal $Y$ forms on the surface of $X$ |
| 2 | $Z(s) + XNO_3(aq)$ | No observable reaction occurs |
| 3 | $W(s) + ZNO_3(aq)$ | Metal $W$ dissolves and the solution changes color |
| 4 | $Y(s) + ZNO_3(aq)$ | No observable reaction occurs |
| 5 | $X(s) + WNO_3(aq)$ | A dark deposit forms on the surface of $X$ |

Based on these results, what is the correct order of the metals from strongest to weakest reducing agent (i.e. from most to least reactive metal, most readily oxidized in these displacement reactions)?

parts.content: Determine the change in the oxidation state of the Mn when the $MnO_4^-$ ion is reduced to the $Mn^{2+}$ ion.
parts.markscheme: +7 to +2 <marks> 1 mark </marks>

steps.title: Identify the goal
steps.content: To find the change in oxidation state, we need to calculate the oxidation number of Manganese (Mn) in both the reactant ($MnO_4^-$) and the product ($Mn^{2+}$). This topic is covered in **Reactivity 3.2: Electron transfer reactions**.

steps.title: Oxidation state of Mn2+
steps.content:
For a monoatomic ion, the oxidation state is simply equal to the charge of the ion. 

Therefore, the oxidation state of Mn in $Mn^{2+}$ is **+2**.

steps.title: Algebraic setup for MnO4-
steps.content:
In a polyatomic ion, the sum of all oxidation states must equal the overall charge of the ion. We use the standard rule that oxygen (O) has an oxidation state of **-2**.

Let $x$ be the oxidation state of Mn in $MnO_4^-$. Since the overall charge is -1, we can set up the following equation:

$$x + 4(-2) = -1$$

steps.title: Solve for Mn
steps.content:
Now, we solve the algebraic equation for $x$:

$$\begin{aligned} x - 8 &= -1 \\ x &= -1 + 8 \\ x &= +7 \end{aligned}$$

So, the oxidation state of Mn in $MnO_4^-$ is **+7**.

gdc.instructions: Perform the addition -1 + 8.

ti84.instructions: Enter the calculation to find x.

desmos.instructions: Evaluate the expression.

calculator.description: Verify the simple addition to ensure the oxidation state is correct.

steps.title: State the final change
steps.content:
The oxidation state of Manganese changes from **+7** in the permanganate ion to **+2** in the manganese(II) ion.

Final Answer: **+7 to +2**

steps.title: Define the species
steps.content: To solve this using **Redox Half-Equation Analysis**, we first identify the chemical species involved in the reaction as stated in the question: the permanganate ion ($MnO_4^-$) is reduced to the manganese(II) ion ($Mn^{2+}$).

steps.title: Reference the half-equation
steps.content:
In an acidic environment, the reduction of the permanganate ion is represented by the following balanced half-equation found in the context of **Topic R3.2: Electron transfer reactions**:

$$MnO_4^-(aq) + 8H^+(aq) + 5e^- \rightarrow Mn^{2+}(aq) + 4H_2O(l)$$

This equation is a fundamental part of redox chemistry, showing exactly how many electrons are transferred to convert the reactant into the product.

steps.title: Analyze electron transfer
steps.content:
From the balanced half-equation, we observe that the process involves the **gain of five electrons** ($5e^-$). 

In redox chemistry, the gain of electrons corresponds to a proportional decrease in the oxidation state. Therefore, a gain of 5 electrons indicates that the oxidation state of Manganese must decrease by 5 units.

steps.title: Determine oxidation states
steps.content:
We can now pinpoint the specific oxidation states:
1. The oxidation state of Mn in the product ($Mn^{2+}$) is **+2** (the charge of the ion).
2. Since there was a gain of 5 electrons, the initial oxidation state of Mn in $MnO_4^-$ must have been **+7**.

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Initial State} - 5e^- &= +2 \\ \text{Initial State} &= +7 \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Input 2 + 5.

ti84.instructions: Add the final state (2) and the number of electrons (5).

desmos.instructions: Type 2 + 5.

calculator.description: Calculate the initial oxidation state by adding the electrons gained back to the final state.

steps.title: State the final change
steps.content: The change in the oxidation state of Manganese is from **+7 to +2**.

parts.content:
The graph below shows the wavelengths of light absorbed by the solution of $MnO_4^-$ ions.

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/plots/39ebaeaa-24f5-4ef7-b8ea-a6140aad561c.png)

Determine the optimum wavelength absorbed by the solution and to which region of the electromagnetic spectrum it belongs.
parts.markscheme:
526 nm <marks> 1 mark </marks>

Visible region <marks> 1 mark </marks>

steps.title: Locate the peak absorbance
steps.content:
In colourimetric analysis, the **optimum wavelength** ($̇\lambda_{max}$) is the wavelength where the substance shows its highest absorbance. This wavelength is chosen for analysis because it provides the highest sensitivity to changes in concentration.

Looking at the provided graph, we need to find the highest point (the peak) of the curve.

steps.title: Read the wavelength value
steps.content:
Once the peak is located, trace a vertical line down to the horizontal axis (the x-axis), which represents the wavelength in nanometers ($nm$).

The peak falls between the $500$ and $550$ $nm$ markings. On a standard IB exam, a small range is usually accepted, but following the peak accurately gives us a value of approximately:

$$\lambda = 526 \text{ nm}$$

steps.title: Identify the EM region
steps.content:
Now we must classify this wavelength within the electromagnetic (EM) spectrum. This concept is covered in **Topic S1.3: Electron configurations**, specifically regarding emission and absorption spectra.

The human eye can detect light with wavelengths roughly between $400 \text{ nm}$ and $700 \text{ nm}$. Since $526 \text{ nm}$ falls squarely within this range, it belongs to the **visible region**.

steps.title: Final answer summary
steps.content:
To earn full marks, you must provide both the numerical value and the correct region of the spectrum:

*   **Optimum wavelength:** $526 \text{ nm}$
*   **Region:** Visible region

parts.content: Calculate the volume, in cm$^3$, of the $2.40 \times 10^{-3}$ mol dm$^{-3}$ stock solution that is required to produce 100 cm$^3$ of a solution with a concentration of $1.68 \times 10^{-3}$ mol dm$^{-3}$.
parts.markscheme:
$n=CV$

$n = 1.68 \times 10^{-3} \times (100/1000)$ <marks> 1 mark </marks>

$n = 1.68 \times 10^{-4}\text{ mol}$ <marks> 1 mark </marks>

$V = 1.68 \times 10^{-4} / 2.40 \times 10^{-3} = 0.07 \text{ dm}^3 = 70 \text{ cm}^3$ <marks> 1 mark </marks>

steps.title: Identify the dilution variables
steps.content:
To solve this using the **dilution formula**, we first identify the given values for our initial (stock) solution and our final (diluted) solution. The number of moles remains constant during dilution, which leads to the formula $$C_1V_1 = C_2V_2$$.

*   **Initial Concentration ($C_1$):** $2.40 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mol dm}^{-3}$
*   **Initial Volume ($V_1$):** ? (This is what we need to find)
*   **Final Concentration ($C_2$):** $1.68 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mol dm}^{-3}$
*   **Final Volume ($V_2$):** $100 \text{ cm}^3$

This relationship is a direct derivation of the molar concentration formula found in your data booklet.

steps.title: Rearrange the dilution formula
steps.content:
We need to find the volume of the stock solution ($V_1$). We can rearrange the equation $$C_1V_1 = C_2V_2$$ to solve for $V_1$:

$$V_1 = \frac{C_2 \times V_2}{C_1}$$

Since both $C_1$ and $C_2$ have the same units ($\text{mol dm}^{-3}$), they will cancel out. This means we can keep $V_2$ in $\text{cm}^3$, and our answer for $V_1$ will automatically be in $\text{cm}^3$.

steps.title: Calculate the required volume
steps.content:
Now, substitute the values into the rearranged equation:

$$V_1 = \frac{(1.68 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mol dm}^{-3}) \times (100 \text{ cm}^3)}{2.40 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mol dm}^{-3}}$$

Notice that the $10^{-3}$ terms in the concentrations cancel out, simplifying the calculation to:

$$V_1 = \frac{1.68 \times 100}{2.40} = 70 \text{ cm}^3$$

calculator.description: Calculate the stock volume directly using the dilution formula.

steps.title: Final answer and marks
steps.content:
The volume of the stock solution required is **$70.0 \text{ cm}^3$**.

In an IB exam, you earn marks as follows:
1.  Identifying the relationship between concentration and volume (even implicitly by setting up the ratio).
2.  Correctly calculating the moles or the proportional ratio ($1.68/2.40$).
3.  Providing the final answer with the correct units ($70 \text{ cm}^3$).

steps.title: Identify the goal
steps.content:
In this problem, we want to find the volume of a concentrated **stock solution** needed to create a specific volume and concentration of a diluted solution. 

We are given:
- Stock concentration ($C_{\text{stock}}$): $2.40 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mol dm}^{-3}$
- Target volume ($V_{\text{target}}$): $100 \text{ cm}^3$
- Target concentration ($C_{\text{target}}$): $1.68 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mol dm}^{-3}$

This falls under **Topic S1.4: The mole** in your syllabus. We'll use the stepwise mole calculation approach, which involves finding the total moles of solute required first.

steps.title: Calculate moles required
steps.content:
First, we need to determine exactly how many moles ($n$) of $MnO_4^-$ are required in our final $100 \text{ cm}^3$ solution. 

From the Data Booklet, we use the concentration formula:

$$n = CV$$

Before calculating, we must convert the target volume from $\text{cm}^3$ to $\text{dm}^3$ to match the units of concentration ($1 \text{ dm}^3 = 1000 \text{ cm}^3$).

$$V_{\text{target}} = \frac{100 \text{ cm}^3}{1000} = 0.100 \text{ dm}^3$$

Now, calculate the moles:

$$n = (1.68 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mol dm}^{-3}) \times (0.100 \text{ dm}^3) = 1.68 \times 10^{-4} \text{ mol}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter the concentration multiplied by the volume in cubic decimeters.

ti84.instructions: Enter the concentration, multiply by the volume (100/1000).

desmos.instructions: Calculate the product of concentration and volume.

calculator.description: Calculate moles required in the final solution.

steps.title: Calculate stock volume
steps.content:
Next, we find what volume of the **stock solution** provides this exact amount of moles. We rearrange $n = CV$ to solve for volume:

$$V = \frac{n}{C}$$

Using the moles calculated in the previous step and the stock concentration:

$$V_{\text{stock}} = \frac{1.68 \times 10^{-4} \text{ mol}}{2.40 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mol dm}^{-3}} = 0.0700 \text{ dm}^3$$

gdc.instructions: Perform the division of moles by stock concentration.

ti84.instructions: Divide the calculated moles by the stock concentration.

desmos.instructions: Divide the total moles by the stock concentration.

calculator.description: Calculate the required volume of the stock solution in dm³.

steps.title: Convert to final units
steps.content:
The question asks for the volume in $\text{cm}^3$. To convert from $\text{dm}^3$ back to $\text{cm}^3$, we multiply by $1000$:

$$V = 0.0700 \text{ dm}^3 \times 1000 = 70.0 \text{ cm}^3$$

**Final Answer:** $70.0 \text{ cm}^3$

gdc.instructions: Multiply by 1000 to convert to cm³.

ti84.instructions: Multiply the result by 1000.

desmos.instructions: Multiply the volume in dm³ by 1000.

calculator.description: Convert the volume from dm³ to cm³.

parts.content:
The student produces the calibration curve shown.

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/plots/c19bd560-dc53-4363-9e34-b7e42ef86cc0.png)

Use the curve to determine the concentration of a solution with an absorbance of 0.45.
parts.markscheme:
Concentration $= 1.25 \times 10^{-3}\text{ mol dm}^{-3}$ <marks> 1 mark </marks>

<note> Allow $1.20 \times 10^{-3} - 1.30 \times 10^{-3}$ </note>

steps.title: Locate absorbance on y-axis
steps.content:
First, we need to find the given value on the vertical axis. The problem provides an absorbance of $0.45$. 

On the y-axis, the markings are every $0.2$ units (e.g., $0.4$, $0.6$). There are $10$ small squares between $0.4$ and $0.6$, so each small square represents $0.02$. To find $0.45$, you should look $2.5$ squares above the $0.4$ mark.

steps.title: Trace to the curve
steps.content: Once you have located $0.45$ on the y-axis, use a ruler to draw a horizontal line straight across until it hits the plotted calibration curve.

steps.title: Trace down to x-axis
steps.content: From the point where your horizontal line meets the curve, draw a vertical line straight down to the x-axis. This axis represents the concentration of $MnO_4^-(aq)$.

steps.title: Read the concentration value
steps.content:
Now, read the value on the x-axis. The line hits between the $1.2$ and $1.3$ marks. Specifically, it lands at $1.25$.

Always check the units and the multiplier on the axis label. The label says: 
$$\text{Concentration} / 10^{-3} \text{ mol dm}^{-3}$$

This means the value we read must be multiplied by $10^{-3}$.

gdc.instructions: Enter the value with the appropriate power of ten.

ti84.instructions: Simply enter the value followed by the scientific notation multiplier.

desmos.instructions: Type the value and the power of ten.

calculator.description: Convert the value from the graph into scientific notation.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The concentration corresponding to an absorbance of $0.45$ is:

$$\text{Concentration} = 1.25 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mol dm}^{-3}$$

According to the markscheme, any value between $1.20 \times 10^{-3}$ and $1.30 \times 10^{-3}$ is acceptable due to the nature of reading from a graph.

steps.title: Identify the linear relationship
steps.content:
The calibration curve shows a direct proportional relationship between absorbance ($A$) and concentration ($c$). This follows the Beer-Lambert Law, which can be expressed in the form of a linear equation passing through the origin:

$$A = m \times c$$

Where $m$ is the gradient (slope) of the line. To find the unknown concentration for an absorbance of 0.45, we first need to determine the value of this gradient.

steps.title: Calculate the gradient
steps.content:
To find the gradient ($m$), we pick a clear point on the line. Let's use the point where the concentration is $2.50 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mol dm}^{-3}$ and the absorbance is $0.90$. 

$$\begin{aligned} m &= \frac{\text{Absorbance}}{\text{Concentration}} \\ m &= \frac{0.90}{2.50 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mol dm}^{-3}} \\ m &= 360 \text{ dm}^{3} \text{ mol}^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Divide the absorbance value by the concentration value.

ti84.instructions: Enter the division to find the gradient.

desmos.instructions: Input the fraction to solve for the gradient.

calculator.description: Calculate the gradient of the calibration curve.

steps.title: Solve for unknown concentration
steps.content:
Now that we have the gradient ($m = 360$), we can use the specific absorbance value given in the question to find the corresponding concentration ($c$):

$$\begin{aligned} A &= m \times c \\ 0.45 &= 360 \times c \\ c &= \frac{0.45}{360} \end{aligned}$$

Performing this calculation gives us the concentration in $\text{mol dm}^{-3}$.

gdc.instructions: Divide 0.45 by 360.

ti84.instructions: Divide 0.45 by 360.

desmos.instructions: Divide 0.45 by 360.

calculator.description: Divide the given absorbance by the gradient to find the concentration.

steps.title: State final answer
steps.content:
The calculation yields $0.00125$. Converting this to scientific notation, we get:

$$c = 1.25 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mol dm}^{-3}$$

This falls exactly within the accepted markscheme range of $1.20 \times 10^{-3}$ to $1.30 \times 10^{-3}$.
