parts.content: Deduce the oxidation numbers of iron in $\mathrm{Fe}^{2+}$ and $\mathrm{Fe}^{3+}$.
parts.markscheme: Oxidation number of $\mathrm{Fe}^{2+} = +2$; $\mathrm{Fe}^{3+} = +3$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Define the Monoatomic Ion Rule
steps.content:
The **Monoatomic Ion Rule** is a fundamental guideline in chemistry (covered in **Topic S3.1.6: Oxidation states**). It states that for any ion consisting of only one atom, its oxidation number is exactly equal to its ionic charge.

In IB Chemistry, remember that we write charges with the number first (e.g., $2+$) and oxidation states with the sign first (e.g., $+2$).

steps.title: Apply rule to $\mathrm{Fe}^{2+}$
steps.content:
We look at the first ion provided in the question: $\mathrm{Fe}^{2+}$. This is a single iron atom with a charge of $2+$.

According to our rule, the oxidation number is equal to the charge:
$$\text{Oxidation number of } \mathrm{Fe}^{2+} = +2$$

steps.title: Apply rule to $\mathrm{Fe}^{3+}$
steps.content:
Next, we look at the second ion: $\mathrm{Fe}^{3+}$. This is a single iron atom with a charge of $3+$.

Applying the same rule:
$$\text{Oxidation number of } \mathrm{Fe}^{3+} = +3$$

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
To earn the mark, ensure you include the positive sign, as oxidation numbers must always show their polarity.

Oxidation number of $\mathrm{Fe}^{2+} = +2$
Oxidation number of $\mathrm{Fe}^{3+} = +3$

steps.title: Define oxidation number logic
steps.content: In chemistry, an oxidation number represents the hypothetical charge an atom would have if all bonds to atoms of different elements were 100% ionic. Using **Electron Transfer Logic**, we determine the oxidation state by looking at how many electrons a neutral atom has lost or gained. For positive ions (cations), the oxidation number is equal to the number of electrons lost from the neutral atom.

steps.title: Analyze the iron(II) ion
steps.content:
To form the $\mathrm{Fe}^{2+}$ ion, a neutral iron atom ($\mathrm{Fe}$) must lose two electrons ($e^-$). 

$$\mathrm{Fe} \rightarrow \mathrm{Fe}^{2+} + 2e^{-}$$ 

Since the loss of electrons results in a positive oxidation state, the loss of 2 electrons corresponds to an oxidation number of $+2$. This concept is covered in **Topic R3.2: Electron transfer reactions**.

steps.title: Analyze the iron(III) ion
steps.content:
Similarly, to form the $\mathrm{Fe}^{3+}$ ion, a neutral iron atom must lose three electrons.

$$\mathrm{Fe} \rightarrow \mathrm{Fe}^{3+} + 3e^{-}$$ 

Following the same logic, the loss of 3 electrons results in an oxidation number of $+3$. Note that in IB Chemistry, we always write the sign ($+$ or $-$) *before* the number for oxidation states, whereas for ion charges, the sign is written *after* the number (e.g., $3+$).

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The oxidation numbers are:
- For $\mathrm{Fe}^{2+}$: $+2$
- For $\mathrm{Fe}^{3+}$: $+3$

This is a standard property of transition metals which can form multiple stable ions by losing different numbers of electrons from their d-subshells, as discussed in **Topic S3.1: The periodic table**.

parts.content: Write a balanced half-equation for the oxidation of $\mathrm{Fe}^{2+}$ to $\mathrm{Fe}^{3+}$.
parts.markscheme:
$\mathrm{Fe}^{2+} \to \mathrm{Fe}^{3+} + \mathrm{e}^-$

- Correct species <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Balanced for charge <marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Identify the redox process
steps.content:
The question asks for the **oxidation** of $\mathrm{Fe}^{2+}$ to $\mathrm{Fe}^{3+}$. 

In redox chemistry, remember the mnemonic **OIL RIG**: **O**xidation **I**s **L**oss of electrons. This means that for iron to be oxidized, it must lose electrons, which will appear on the product (right) side of our equation.

steps.title: Check the mass balance
steps.content:
Before balancing the charge, we must ensure the atoms (mass) are balanced. 

We start with one iron ion on the reactant side and end with one iron ion on the product side:

$$\mathrm{Fe}^{2+} \rightarrow \mathrm{Fe}^{3+}$$

Since there is 1 iron atom on both sides, the mass is already balanced.

steps.title: Balance the net charge
steps.content:
Now we look at the total charge on each side:
- **Left side (reactants):** $+2$
- **Right side (products):** $+3$

To balance the charges, we add electrons ($e^-$), which each have a charge of $-1$. We add them to the more positive side until the charges are equal.

$$+2 = +3 + (-1)$$

By adding one electron to the product side, the total charge on both sides becomes $+2$.

steps.title: Final balanced half-equation
steps.content:
Combining everything, we get the final balanced half-equation:

$$\mathrm{Fe}^{2+} \rightarrow \mathrm{Fe}^{3+} + \mathrm{e}^{-}$$

According to the markscheme, you earn 1 mark for the correct species and 1 mark for balancing the charge correctly.

steps.title: Identify the reaction type
steps.content:
The question asks for a balanced half-equation for the **oxidation** of $\mathrm{Fe}^{2+}$ to $\mathrm{Fe}^{3+}$. 

In redox chemistry, oxidation is defined as the **loss of electrons**. This means the reactant will be $\mathrm{Fe}^{2+}$, and the products will include $\mathrm{Fe}^{3+}$ and at least one electron. This is covered in **Topic R3.2: Electron transfer reactions**.

steps.title: Locate the reduction potential
steps.content:
To find the correct species and electron count, we look at the standard reduction potentials in the IB Data Booklet. Half-equations in the booklet are always written as **reductions** (gain of electrons).

You will find the following entry:

$$\mathrm{Fe}^{3+}(\mathrm{aq}) + \mathrm{e}^- \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{Fe}^{2+}(\mathrm{aq})$$

steps.title: Reverse for oxidation
steps.content:
Because the question specifically asks for the **oxidation** process, we must reverse the reduction equation from the Data Booklet. 

When we reverse the equation, the products become the reactants and vice-versa:

$$\mathrm{Fe}^{2+}(\mathrm{aq}) \to \mathrm{Fe}^{3+}(\mathrm{aq}) + \mathrm{e}^-$$

steps.title: Verify balance and marks
steps.content:
Finally, let's check the balance to ensure we get both marks:
1. **Species Check**: We have $\mathrm{Fe}^{2+}$ on the left and $\mathrm{Fe}^{3+}$ on the right. (1 mark)
2. **Charge Check**: 
   - Left side: $+2$
   - Right side: $+3 + (-1) = +2$
The charges are equal, so it is balanced. (1 mark)

**Final Answer:**
$$\mathrm{Fe}^{2+} \to \mathrm{Fe}^{3+} + \mathrm{e}^-$$

parts.content: Explain why iron, as a transition metal, can function as both an oxidizing and a reducing agent.
parts.markscheme:
- $\mathrm{Fe}^{2+}$ can lose an electron, acting as a reducing agent <marks>1 mark</marks>
- $\mathrm{Fe}^{3+}$ can gain an electron, acting as an oxidizing agent <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Iron's ability to change oxidation states enables it to participate in both redox roles <marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Identify transition metal properties
steps.content:
To understand why iron plays a dual role, we first look at its nature as a transition metal. Transition metals are characterized by their ability to form stable ions with different oxidation states. 

In the case of iron, it commonly exists as $\mathrm{Fe}^{2+}$ and $\mathrm{Fe}^{3+}$. This flexibility is what allows it to either donate or accept electrons. This concept is a key part of **Topic S3.1.9: Oxidation states of transition elements**.

steps.title: Analyze Fe2+ as a reducer
steps.content:
A **reducing agent** is a species that donates electrons to another substance, becoming oxidized in the process. 

Iron(II) ions ($\mathrm{Fe}^{2+}$) can lose one electron to form the more stable Iron(III) ion ($\mathrm{Fe}^{3+}$):

$$\mathrm{Fe}^{2+} \rightarrow \mathrm{Fe}^{3+} + \mathrm{e}^{-}$$ 

Because it can lose this electron, $\mathrm{Fe}^{2+}$ acts as a reducing agent.

highlights.text: Fe2+ can lose an electron, acting as a reducing agent

steps.title: Analyze Fe3+ as an oxidizer
steps.content:
Conversely, an **oxidizing agent** is a species that accepts electrons from another substance, becoming reduced in the process. 

Iron(III) ions ($\mathrm{Fe}^{3+}$) can gain an electron to return to the Iron(II) state:

$$\mathrm{Fe}^{3+} + \mathrm{e}^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{Fe}^{2+}$$ 

By gaining an electron, $\mathrm{Fe}^{3+}$ acts as an oxidizing agent.

highlights.text: Fe3+ can gain an electron, acting as an oxidizing agent

steps.title: Synthesize the dual role
steps.content:
By combining these two abilities, we can see that iron functions as a "redox bridge." Its capacity to switch between the $+2$ and $+3$ oxidation states means it can participate in a wide variety of chemical environments, either providing electrons to a system or removing them. 

This interconversion between oxidation states is the fundamental reason why it can perform both roles in biological systems (like in hemoglobin or cytochromes) and industrial catalysts.

highlights.text: Iron's ability to change oxidation states enables it to participate in both redox roles

steps.title: Variable oxidation states
steps.content: Transition metals like iron are unique because they can exist in multiple stable oxidation states. This is a characteristic property resulting from the small energy difference between the $4s$ and $3d$ orbitals. For iron, the most common stable states are $+2$ and $+3$. This flexibility allows iron to either donate or accept electrons depending on the reaction environment.

steps.title: Iron as a reducing agent
steps.content:
A reducing agent is a species that loses electrons (it is oxidized). The $\text{Fe}^{2+}$ ion can be oxidized to $\text{Fe}^{3+}$ by losing one electron:

$$\text{Fe}^{2+} \rightarrow \text{Fe}^{3+} + \text{e}^{-}$$

By donating this electron to another species, $\text{Fe}^{2+}$ acts as a **reducing agent**.

steps.title: Iron as an oxidizing agent
steps.content:
An oxidizing agent is a species that gains electrons (it is reduced). The $\text{Fe}^{3+}$ ion can be reduced back to $\text{Fe}^{2+}$ by gaining one electron:

$$\text{Fe}^{3+} + \text{e}^{-} \rightarrow \text{Fe}^{2+}$$

By accepting an electron from another species, $\text{Fe}^{3+}$ acts as an **oxidizing agent**.

steps.title: Summary of redox roles
steps.content: In summary, iron's ability to easily transition between the $+2$ and $+3$ oxidation states enables it to perform dual roles. It can act as a reducing agent when in the lower oxidation state and as an oxidizing agent when in the higher oxidation state, making it vital for industrial and biological electron transfer processes.

steps.title: Define the half-equation
steps.content:
To understand how iron acts as both an agent of oxidation and reduction, we look at the reversible half-equation involving its most common ions, $\mathrm{Fe}^{2+}$ and $\mathrm{Fe}^{3+}$. This concept is a key part of **Topic R3.2: Electron transfer reactions**.

$$\mathrm{Fe}^{2+}(\text{aq}) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{Fe}^{3+}(\text{aq}) + \mathrm{e}^{-}$$

steps.title: Analyze the reducing role
steps.content:
In the forward direction of our equation, the $\mathrm{Fe}^{2+}$ ion loses an electron to become $\mathrm{Fe}^{3+}$:

$$\mathrm{Fe}^{2+} \rightarrow \mathrm{Fe}^{3+} + \mathrm{e}^{-}$$ 

Because $\mathrm{Fe}^{2+}$ is losing an electron (oxidation), it provides that electron to another substance, causing that substance to be reduced. Therefore, **$\mathrm{Fe}^{2+}$ acts as a reducing agent**.

highlights.text: Fe2+ can lose an electron, acting as a reducing agent

steps.title: Analyze the oxidizing role
steps.content:
In the reverse direction, the $\mathrm{Fe}^{3+}$ ion gains an electron to return to the $\mathrm{Fe}^{2+}$ state:

$$\mathrm{Fe}^{3+} + \mathrm{e}^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{Fe}^{2+}$$

Because $\mathrm{Fe}^{3+}$ is gaining an electron (reduction), it takes that electron from another substance, causing that substance to be oxidized. Therefore, **$\mathrm{Fe}^{3+}$ acts as an oxidizing agent**.

highlights.text: Fe3+ can gain an electron, acting as an oxidizing agent

steps.title: Connect to transition metals
steps.content:
The reason iron can do this is due to a characteristic property of transition metals: **variable oxidation states**. 

Because iron can stably exist as both $+2$ and $+3$ ions, it has the flexibility to either donate or accept electrons depending on the reaction conditions. This ability to cycle between states is what allows it to function in both redox roles.

highlights.text: Iron's ability to change oxidation states enables it to participate in both redox roles

questions.specification:
An industrial chemist monitors the production of chlorine gas in an electrolytic cell containing an aqueous solution of a metal chloride, $X\text{Cl}_2$. During a test run, a constant current of $0.750\text{ A}$ was passed through the cell. At the anode, $360\text{ cm}^3$ of chlorine gas was collected, measured at room temperature and pressure (RTP). Simultaneously, $0.953\text{ g}$ of metal $X$ was deposited on the cathode.

(Molar volume of a gas at RTP $= 24.0\text{ dm}^3\text{ mol}^{-1}$; Faraday constant $F = 96500\text{ C mol}^{-1}$)

parts.content: Calculate the amount, in mol, of chlorine gas produced at the anode during the electrolysis.
parts.markscheme:
- Convert the gas volume to $\text{dm}^3$: $V = \dfrac{360}{1000} = 0.360\text{ dm}^3$ <marks>1 mark</marks>
- State or use the molar volume relationship: $n(\text{Cl}_2) = \dfrac{V}{V_m}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Calculate the final value: $n(\text{Cl}_2) = \dfrac{0.360\text{ dm}^3}{24.0\text{ dm}^3\text{ mol}^{-1}} = 0.0150\text{ mol}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award 3 marks for the correct final answer.</note>

parts.content: State the half-equation for the formation of chlorine gas at the anode.
parts.markscheme:
- $2\text{Cl}^-(aq) \rightarrow \text{Cl}_2(g) + 2e^-$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Accept equations showing $e^-$ on the left if the charge is balanced correctly, but this is the standard oxidation representation.</note>

parts.content: Determine the total amount, in mol, of electrons that passed through the circuit during this process.
parts.markscheme:
- Identify the $1:2$ ratio between $\text{Cl}_2$ and $e^-$ from the half-equation: $n(e^-) = 2 \times n(\text{Cl}_2)$ <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Calculate the amount of electrons: $2 \times 0.0150\text{ mol} = 0.0300\text{ mol}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Calculate the duration of the electrolysis process in minutes.
parts.markscheme:
- Calculate the total charge passed: $Q = n(e^-) \times F = 0.0300\text{ mol} \times 96500\text{ C mol}^{-1} = 2895\text{ C}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Calculate the time in seconds: $t = \dfrac{Q}{I} = \dfrac{2895\text{ C}}{0.750\text{ A}} = 3860\text{ s}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Convert the time to minutes: $\dfrac{3860}{60} = 64.3\text{ min}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Calculate the relative atomic mass of metal $X$ and identify the metal using the data provided.
parts.markscheme:
- Determine the moles of $X$ deposited (using $X^{2+} + 2e^- \rightarrow X$): $n(X) = \dfrac{0.0300}{2} = 0.0150\text{ mol}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Calculate the molar mass and identify the metal: $M = \dfrac{0.953\text{ g}}{0.0150\text{ mol}} = 63.53\text{ g mol}^{-1}$. Therefore, $X$ is Copper/$\text{Cu}$. <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification: Which element has the same oxidation number in both species?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Structure 3. Classification of matter > S3.1 The periodic table: Classification of elements > S3.1.6 Oxidation states
labeledSubSubTopics.title: S3.1.6 Oxidation states
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks to compare oxidation numbers of elements in different species (ions/compounds), which is directly the skill of assigning and interpreting oxidation states.

options.markscheme: In $\text{NH}_4^+$, Nitrogen is $-3$. In $\text{NO}_2^-$, Nitrogen is $+3$.
In $\text{Cr}_2\text{O}_7^{2-}$, Chromium is $+6$. In $\text{CrO}_3$, Chromium is $+6$.
In $\text{KO}_2$ (superoxide), Oxygen is $-\frac{1}{2}$. In $\text{K}_2\text{O}$ (oxide), Oxygen is $-2$.
In $\text{H}_2\text{O}$, Hydrogen is $+1$. In $\text{LiH}$ (metal hydride), Hydrogen is $-1$.
